proposals.title: Scaling Laws Across Tasks and Architectures: A Systematic Analysis of Validation Performance vs. Computation
proposals.motivation: Recent work, including the OLMo project, has established scaling laws for language model pretraining, but these analyses are primarily confined to autoregressive language modeling loss on decoder-only transformers. The field lacks a systematic understanding of how these scaling relationships generalize to a wider range of downstream NLP tasks and diverse model architectures. This limits our ability to predict the computational requirements for achieving target performance on specific applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the power-law relationship between validation performance and compute is a general phenomenon, but that its specific parameters (the exponent and coefficient) vary predictably based on task difficulty, data domain, and architectural choices.
proposals.method: We will leverage the open-source OLMo framework and the Dolma corpus to train a controlled suite of models of varying scales (from 100M to 10B parameters). We will extend the analysis beyond decoder-only transformers to include encoder-decoder (T5-style) and encoder-only (BERT-style) architectures. For each model, we will measure validation performance not only on pretraining loss but also on a curated benchmark of diverse tasks (e.g., reasoning, code generation, summarization, classification) from the LM Evaluation Harness, plotting performance against FLOPs consumed during training.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a public dataset of task-specific scaling curves, providing a crucial resource for the community to estimate the compute budget needed for application-specific goals. It will advance the science of language models by testing the universality of scaling laws and identifying architectural and task-based factors that modulate efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws for pretraining loss are known, a systematic, multi-architecture, multi-task analysis using a fully open and reproducible pipeline (OLMo/Dolma) to disentangle the effects of compute, architecture, and task type has not been conducted.

proposals.title: Debiasing Data Filters: A Controlled Study of Cleaning Effects on English vs. Non-English Web Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current large language model (LLM) pretraining pipelines, such as those used for models like OLMo, often apply aggressive, heuristic-based cleaning filters (e.g., for quality, toxicity, or boilerplate) developed and tuned primarily on English web data. This practice risks systematically degrading the quality and diversity of non-English pretraining corpora, like subsets of Dolma, by removing culturally or linguistically normative content misclassified as 'noise.' This bias perpetuates the performance gap between English and multilingual models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the perceived noisiness of non-English web text is an artifact of filter design, not an intrinsic property of the data. Applying filters tuned for English will remove a disproportionately higher amount of linguistically valid and informative content from non-English corpora compared to English corpora of comparable intrinsic quality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled, language-wise ablation study using the open Dolma toolkit and corpus. For a stratified sample of languages (e.g., Spanish, Swahili, Hindi, Chinese), we will create parallel 'clean' subsets by applying standard English-tuned filters (e.g., from CCNet or C4) and alternative, minimally restrictive baselines (e.g., only deduplication). We will then pretrain medium-scale OLMo-style models on each subset and evaluate them on language-specific perplexity and a suite of tasks from datasets like FLORES-101 and XNLI. Crucially, we will use the AboutMe-style self-description analysis to manually audit and categorize removed content, quantifying the fraction of valid linguistic material erroneously filtered per language.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven audit of cleaning bias in multilingual LLM pretraining, yielding concrete metrics on filter-induced data loss per language. It will contribute reproducible, open-source code for multilingual corpus analysis and propose evidence-based guidelines for less destructive, more equitable filtering strategies. The findings will directly inform the science of language models by challenging a core, untested assumption in data curation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., AboutMe) has analyzed filter effects in English, no study has systematically compared the impact of standard filters across languages using controlled pretraining experiments. This moves beyond documenting composition to measuring the causal impact of curation choices on model capabilities for non-English languages.

proposals.title: REP-Corpus: A Framework for Constructing and Evaluating Representative, Not Just Cleaned, Web-Scale Pretraining Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current large language model (LLM) pretraining corpora, like those in the Dolma lineage, heavily prioritize 'cleaning' via deduplication, quality filtering, and toxicity removal. While well-intentioned, these filters systematically remove content from marginalized demographics and niche topics, creating models that are biased toward a sanitized, majority perspective. This project addresses the critical need for corpora that represent the internet's true demographic and topical diversity to build more equitable and robust models.
proposals.hypothesis: A pretraining corpus constructed with the explicit goal of demographic and topical representativeness, rather than cleanliness, will produce language models with reduced demographic bias in downstream evaluations and improved performance on tasks requiring knowledge of niche or non-standard domains.
proposals.method: We will extend the data curation pipeline from Dolma and OLMo. First, we will develop and apply classifiers (e.g., fine-tuned BERT-style models) to categorize documents by inferred demographic attributes (using author self-descriptions as in AboutMe) and topical categories. Instead of filtering, we will implement stratified sampling to construct a corpus that matches estimated global internet user demographics and topic prevalence. We will pretrain a 1B-parameter OLMo-style model on this REP-Corpus and compare it to a baseline model trained on a 'cleaned' corpus of equal size, evaluating on bias benchmarks (e.g., CrowS-Pairs) and specialized domain tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, open framework and toolkit for auditing and constructing representative corpora, 2) the REP-Corpus itself as a public resource for research, and 3) empirical evidence quantifying the trade-offs between data 'cleanliness' and model representativeness, directly informing the science of language model data curation.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the core objective of modern LLM data curation from exclusion-based cleaning to inclusion-based representation, and provides a methodological framework to operationalize this goal using pre-2026 tools, moving beyond abstract calls for diversity.

proposals.title: The Long-Tail Cost of Filtering: Quantifying the Impact of Pretraining Data Curation on Model Fairness and Capability
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are trained on web-scale corpora filtered for quality, safety, and deduplication, but these filters may disproportionately remove text associated with minority demographics, dialects, and subcultures. This systematic bias in the pretraining corpus, as hinted at by research like AboutMe, risks encoding representational harms and performance disparities into models from the outset, undermining their fairness and utility. This study aims to move from anecdotal evidence to a rigorous, causal quantification of these long-term impacts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of standard pretraining data filters (e.g., for toxicity, quality, or pornography) introduces a measurable and causally significant bias, degrading model performance on tasks related to underrepresented groups more severely than on majority-associated tasks, and that this effect persists even after instruction tuning or reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF).
proposals.method: Leveraging the OLMo framework and the open Dolma corpus, we will create controlled, filtered variants of a large dataset, systematically ablating specific filter categories (e.g., toxicity lists, quality classifiers). We will then pretrain medium-scale OLMo models on each variant and evaluate them on a curated benchmark spanning standard capabilities (MMLU, Hellaswag) and fairness-focused tasks (e.g., dialectal understanding, cultural knowledge QA, bias probes). We will use causal mediation analysis to trace performance disparities back to specific filtering decisions and corpus-level changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, causal evidence linking specific data filtering choices to downstream fairness deficits, offering actionable insights for more equitable corpus construction. It will contribute a public, reproducible benchmark for auditing pretraining data impacts and release model checkpoints that explicitly document filtering effects, advancing the science of transparent and accountable language model development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Dodge et al.'s AboutMe) has documented filter disparities in the data, this study uniquely establishes a direct, causal link from those disparities to quantifiable performance harms in fully trained models, using the controlled, open-source OLMo toolkit to ensure reproducibility and isolate variables.

proposals.title: Provenance Profiles: A Comparative Analysis of Document-Level Metadata in The Pile and OSCAR
proposals.motivation: Large-scale web corpora like The Pile and OSCAR are foundational for modern language model training, yet their composition and the effects of their construction pipelines are often opaque. As shown in work like AboutMe, understanding the provenance of training data—its source, quality, and potential biases—is critical for scientific reproducibility and responsible model development. Extending this documentation analysis beyond English-centric web crawls to these major, diverse datasets is a necessary next step for the open science of language models.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying systematic provenance analysis will reveal significant, previously undocumented heterogeneity in source distribution, quality signals, and potential contamination within and between The Pile and OSCAR, which correlates with measurable differences in downstream model performance on specific tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the methodology from AboutMe, developing scalable scripts to extract and categorize header metadata, URL patterns, and boilerplate from document samples across all subsets of The Pile and OSCAR. For each dataset, we will compute provenance profiles quantifying source types (e.g., academic, news, forum), geographical and linguistic origin via top-level domains, and document quality indicators. We will then pretrain medium-scale OLMo-style models on stratified data slices defined by these profiles to test for performance correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, publicly available provenance profiles for The Pile and OSCAR, enabling more informed dataset selection and composition for researchers. It will contribute a reusable, open-source code framework for dataset documentation and provide empirical evidence linking specific data provenance characteristics to model capabilities and limitations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed single corpora (e.g., AboutMe for C4), a systematic comparative provenance study of these two architecturally and linguistically distinct mega-corpora has not been conducted. The explicit linkage of provenance profiles to controlled, small-scale training experiments moves beyond descriptive analysis to causal hypothesis testing.

proposals.title: Contaminated Corpus: Measuring the Impact of Machine-Translated and Synthetic Text on Language Model Training and Evaluation
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are trained on ever-larger web-scale corpora, the proportion of machine-generated text (e.g., machine-translated content, model outputs) within these datasets is increasing. This creates a potential feedback loop where models are trained on their own or other models' outputs, which may degrade performance, amplify biases, and confound evaluation. Understanding this 'data contamination' is critical for the science of language models, as it threatens the validity of benchmarks and the integrity of future training cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the inclusion of machine-translated and other synthetic text in pretraining data leads to measurable degradation in model capabilities, including reduced factual accuracy, increased hallucination, and superficial, 'translationese' stylistic patterns, particularly for low-resource languages.
proposals.method: We will extend the Dolma curation toolkit to identify and tag synthetic text spans within large corpora, using classifier-based detection and provenance tracing from sources like Common Crawl. Using the OLMo framework, we will train a series of 1-7B parameter models on controlled mixtures of the Pile and Dolma, where we systematically vary the type (e.g., NMT output, LLM-generated text) and proportion of synthetic contamination. We will evaluate these models on a suite of tasks including MMLU, TruthfulQA, BLEURT for translation quality, and novel 'data provenance' probes to measure memorization of synthetic artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) an open-source tool for detecting synthetic text in training corpora, 2) a controlled, open dataset (a 'contamination atlas') documenting the effects of different contamination types, and 3) a rigorous, reproducible analysis of how synthetic data influences model capabilities and evaluation, directly informing future corpus curation practices for the open LLM community.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied test-set contamination, we systematically investigate *training-data* contamination from machine-generated sources, using the controlled, open-source OLMo training framework to establish causal relationships—a novel experimental paradigm for data-centric language model research.

proposals.title: SyntheticWeb: A Framework for Generating Controllable, Web-Mimetic Corpora for Language Model Training
proposals.motivation: Large-scale web corpora like Dolma are foundational for modern language model pretraining, but their composition is a complex, opaque function of web scraping and filtering heuristics. This opacity makes it difficult to conduct controlled experiments on how specific data attributes (e.g., genre mix, toxicity levels, or demographic representation) affect model capabilities and biases. A method to generate synthetic corpora that statistically mimic the web while allowing precise control over these attributes would enable a new science of data ablation and causal analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative framework, trained to capture the multi-scale statistical properties of a real web corpus (e.g., Dolma), can produce high-quality synthetic text that, when used for LM training, yields models with similar downstream performance, while enabling controlled experiments impossible with the original data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will use the OLMo framework to train a suite of small, specialized language models on the Dolma corpus to act as 'corpus generators,' each capturing distributions of specific attributes (e.g., domain, style, quality). Second, we will build a controller that samples from these generators according to a target specification, stitching outputs into a coherent synthetic corpus. We will validate by training OLMo-architecture models on the synthetic data and comparing their performance on benchmarks (e.g., knowledge probes, fairness evaluations) to models trained on the original, filtered Dolma subsets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) an open-source framework for generating controllable, web-mimetic synthetic corpora, 2) a systematic analysis of which corpus statistics are most critical for replicating pretraining outcomes, and 3) a novel methodology for 'inverse data engineering' that enhances the reproducibility and interpretability of large-scale LM training research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents web corpus properties (AboutMe) or builds open corpora (Dolma), the inverse problem of *synthesizing* a corpus with controlled attributes to match those documented properties is novel. This moves beyond filtering and curation to active generation for experimental design.

proposals.title: Data-Efficient Language Model Pre-training via Corpus Curation and Curriculum Learning
proposals.motivation: The current paradigm for training state-of-the-art language models relies on assembling massive, centralized datasets like Dolma, which incurs significant energy, computational, and ethical costs for data collection and filtering. This creates a high barrier to entry for rigorous, reproducible science of language models (as in the OLMo framework) and centralizes influence over model capabilities. A method to achieve strong performance with less data would democratize research and reduce environmental impact.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic, quality-focused curation of smaller, diverse datasets combined with data-efficient training curricula can match or exceed the performance of models trained on orders-of-magnitude larger, indiscriminately filtered corpora.
proposals.method: We will build on the OLMo framework and Dolma's open tools to create a series of carefully curated, multi-domain sub-corpora, informed by data documentation insights from AboutMe. We will test data-efficient training algorithms, including (1) curriculum learning that sequences data from high-quality, trusted sources (e.g., textbooks, code) to noisier web data, and (2) sample-efficient architectures like mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Performance will be benchmarked against baseline OLMo models trained on the full Dolma corpus using standardized, reproducible evaluation suites.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, open-source framework for data-efficient LM pre-training, complete with new curated datasets and training code. It will establish empirical scaling laws for data quality versus quantity, offering concrete guidelines for sustainable model development. The project directly advances the science of language models by challenging the necessity of extreme dataset scale.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on scaling data volume or applying post-hoc filters, this proposal integrates proactive, domain-aware curation with novel training curricula specifically designed for small-data regimes, a relatively unexplored path for modern decoder-only LMs. It uniquely combines Dodge's expertise in open corpora, data documentation, and reproducible model training to tackle data efficiency holistically.

proposals.title: Adaptive Multilingual Training: A Data-Driven Framework for Language-Specific Learning Schedules
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual language models are typically trained with uniform sampling from a corpus like Dolma, ignoring vast disparities in data quality, script complexity, and morphological richness across languages. This uniform approach can lead to suboptimal performance, especially for lower-resource languages, and fails to account for the varying learning dynamics of each language during training. Addressing this is crucial for building more equitable and capable open models, aligning with the OLMo initiative's goal of advancing the science of language modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic, adaptive training schedule, which prioritizes per-language learning needs based on real-time loss metrics, will lead to more efficient pretraining and improved overall and per-language performance compared to static, uniform sampling.
proposals.method: We will extend the OLMo training framework to implement a controller that monitors language-specific loss curves on a held-out validation set. Using this signal, we will adaptively adjust the sampling probability for each language in the Dolma corpus via a multi-armed bandit algorithm (e.g., EXP3) or a proportional scheduler that increases sampling for languages with higher, plateauing loss. We will rigorously test this against a uniform baseline, measuring final performance on a diverse suite of tasks (e.g., from the BIG-bench Hard) and analyzing per-language convergence rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a reproducible, open-source framework for adaptive multilingual training, providing empirical evidence on the benefits of non-uniform schedules. It will yield new insights into the learning trajectories of different languages in large-scale models and offer practical strategies for improving the efficiency of pretraining on massively multilingual corpora.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and adaptive sampling exist, applying them at the granularity of *languages* in a trillion-token corpus, driven by online loss signals rather than pre-defined heuristics, is a novel and data-driven approach to multilingual training science.

proposals.title: ROOTS Analysis: Tracing Model Behaviors and Biases to Corpus Subcomponents
proposals.motivation: Large language models are trained on massive, heterogeneous corpora like ROOTS, but the causal link between specific data sources and emergent model behaviors remains opaque. As the field moves towards open datasets (e.g., Dolma) and models (e.g., OLMo), understanding this link is critical for diagnosing biases, improving data curation, and enabling reproducible science. This work directly addresses the need for transparency in how training data composition shapes model outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific behavioral traits and biases in a language model can be directly attributed to, and predicted by, the proportional representation and linguistic properties of distinct subcorpora within its training mixture.
proposals.method: We will train a suite of medium-scale OLMo-style models, each on a different controlled mixture of subcorpora from the ROOTS corpus (e.g., varying proportions of academic papers, web content, code, and books). Using the methodology from AboutMe, we will design targeted probes and counterfactual datasets to measure how model performance, stylistic outputs, and social biases shift with mixture composition. We will employ causal mediation analysis and linear attribution methods to trace specific model behaviors back to their dominant data sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a detailed map linking dataset ingredients to model behaviors, providing actionable insights for dataset curation. It will deliver a reproducible framework for auditing training data effects, advancing the science of transparent and accountable language model development as championed by the OLMo project.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents aggregate dataset contents, this study proposes a controlled experimental paradigm to establish causal, not just correlational, relationships between specific data sources and model behaviors, moving beyond descriptive analysis to predictive attribution.

proposals.title: Quantifying Corpus Diversity: An Ecological Analysis of Language and Source Distribution in Large-Scale Pretraining Data
proposals.motivation: The composition of pretraining corpora is a critical, yet poorly quantified, factor influencing model capabilities and biases. Current filtering and curation practices, as studied in works like AboutMe and Dolma, often lack principled metrics to assess the diversity and balance of linguistic 'species' (languages, dialects, genres) and data 'ecosystems' (source domains). This gap hinders reproducible science and the intentional design of corpora for specific model behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying established biodiversity metrics from computational ecology (e.g., Shannon entropy, Simpson's index, Gini-Simpson index) to the taxonomic classification of data sources and languages within a corpus will provide actionable, quantitative measures of diversity that correlate with known model performance characteristics on multilingual and domain-specific tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a taxonomy for sources in open corpora like Dolma and Pile, categorizing by language (using fastText), web domain, and content genre. For each corpus and its subsets, we will compute ecological alpha-diversity (within-corpus) and beta-diversity (between-corpus) metrics. We will then analyze the correlation between these diversity indices and the performance of a suite of fixed-architecture OLMo models, trained on intentionally varied subsets, across benchmarks probing multilingual understanding, domain adaptation, and social bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with a standardized, open-source toolkit for measuring and reporting corpus diversity, moving beyond token counts. It will yield concrete, data-driven recommendations for balancing corpora to achieve desired model properties, directly advancing the science of language model pretraining as outlined in the OLMo framework.
proposals.novelty: While dataset analysis is common, the systematic application of formal ecological diversity indices to quantify the composition of pretraining data is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive statistics to offer a rigorous, borrowed-from-another-field framework for understanding data as an ecosystem.

proposals.title: Federated Multilingual Language Model Training: Preserving Data Sovereignty and Improving Low-Resource Language Performance
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale language model pretraining, as exemplified by efforts like Dolma and OLMo, requires centralizing massive, diverse datasets, raising significant privacy, copyright, and data sovereignty concerns, especially for sensitive or regionally specific language data. This centralization bottleneck also limits the diversity and freshness of training data for many languages, hindering progress on equitable, multilingual AI. Federated learning offers a paradigm to train on distributed data without raw data ever leaving its local jurisdiction, directly addressing these legal and ethical challenges while potentially accessing novel data sources.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, using a carefully designed aggregation strategy and local curriculum, can train a multilingual language model that achieves performance parity with centrally-trained baselines on high-resource languages while significantly improving performance on low-resource and dialectal variants by leveraging localized, non-centralizable data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the OLMo architecture and training framework for federated training. The method involves deploying identical model weights to multiple geographically distributed clients, each holding a local corpus (e.g., a specific language or regional text collection). Clients will perform local training steps (e.g., using the OLMo codebase) on their data, and a central server will aggregate model updates using the FedAvg algorithm, with modifications for handling heterogeneous data distributions and client availability. We will develop a sampling strategy to balance contributions across languages and implement rigorous reproducibility tooling to track data provenance and training dynamics, akin to the documentation ethos of the AboutMe project.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, reproducible federated training framework and benchmark for multilingual LMs, extending the open science principles of OLMo; 2) an analysis of the trade-offs between federated and centralized training for language modeling, focusing on performance, privacy, and data efficiency; and 3) a new open, federated-ready dataset specification for multilingual research that documents data sources without centralizing the data itself.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning is established for vision and healthcare, its application to large-scale, causal language model pretraining on truly heterogeneous text data remains largely unexplored. The novelty lies in adapting these methods to the scale and linguistic diversity of projects like Dolma, tackling challenges unique to NLP such as vocabulary mismatches and per-language learning dynamics within a federated system.

proposals.title: Minimally Viable Multilingualism: A Data-Centric Study on Bootstrapping Language Models with Minimal Curated Data
proposals.motivation: Large-scale multilingual models like BLOOM are trained on massive, often opaque datasets, creating significant resource and reproducibility barriers. This work addresses a critical inverse problem: instead of scaling up, we ask what is the minimal, high-quality data required to achieve foundational multilingual capabilities. Determining this lower bound is essential for making multilingual LM research more accessible, interpretable, and efficient.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a small, strategically curated dataset—orders of magnitude smaller than typical pretraining corpora—can bootstrap a model to achieve non-trivial, generalizable multilingual understanding and generation when trained with appropriate curriculum learning and data augmentation techniques.
proposals.method: We will build upon the OLMo framework and Dolma's curation principles to construct a series of minimal 'seed' datasets, varying size (e.g., 1M-100M tokens) and composition (balanced vs. skewed language distributions). Using the OLMo architecture (e.g., 1B parameters), we will pretrain models from scratch on these seeds, employing aggressive data augmentation via back-translation and code-switching. We will rigorously evaluate on a suite of tasks from the BLOOM evaluation framework, comparing performance against models trained on the full Dolma-scale corpus.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a publicly released benchmark of minimal multilingual datasets and trained model checkpoints, advancing open science; 2) a quantitative analysis of the data efficiency frontier for multilingual learning; and 3) practical guidelines for data curation in resource-constrained settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., AboutMe, Dolma) documents the effects of data at scale, this proposal inverts the paradigm to systematically search for a data *minimum*. It combines the reproducibility focus of OLMo with a novel, constrained optimization perspective on multilingual pretraining data.

proposals.title: DualBERT: A Dual-Encoder Model for Corpus-Level Attribution of Language Model Outputs
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) like OLMo are trained on massive, heterogeneous corpora such as Dolma and ROOTS, understanding the provenance of model outputs is critical for transparency, debugging biases, and ensuring reproducibility. Current methods lack scalable, fine-grained techniques to attribute specific LM generations back to their likely source documents or sub-corpora, hindering scientific analysis of training data effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-encoder model, trained to contrastively associate synthetic LM outputs with their originating training document snippets, can learn robust representations for accurate corpus-level attribution, even for outputs not verbatim memorized.
proposals.method: We will construct a training dataset by sampling documents from the Dolma/ROOTS corpus, generating synthetic outputs from an OLMo model conditioned on these documents, and creating positive/negative pairs. We will train a BERT-based dual-encoder model using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) to maximize similarity between an output and its source document embedding while minimizing similarity with other documents. The model will be evaluated on its ability to rank true source documents against distractors for held-out generations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first scalable tool for directly attributing LM outputs to broad pre-training sources, advancing the science of data documentation. It will contribute a novel dataset and model to the open ecosystem, enabling new analyses of how specific corpora influence model behavior and supporting more reproducible language model research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., AboutMe) studies corpus effects statistically, and retrieval-augmented generation retrieves for conditioning, this model is novel in being trained specifically for the inverse problem: diagnosing the training data sources of existing model outputs post-hoc.

proposals.title: M3D: A Massive, Multilingual, and Multimodal Open Dataset for Language Model Pre-training
proposals.motivation: The rapid advancement of language models has been fueled by large, English-centric text corpora, which risks encoding cultural and linguistic biases while limiting global utility. While open datasets like Dolma have increased transparency, they lack significant multilingual and multimodal content, which is crucial for developing models that can process the world's diverse information. Creating a truly open, large-scale, and varied dataset is a prerequisite for the next generation of equitable and capable AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully constructed, massively multilingual and multimodal open dataset, built with reproducible and documented filtering pipelines, will enable the training of language models with significantly improved cross-lingual understanding and multimodal reasoning capabilities, as measured by established benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the reproducible data curation pipelines from Dolma and OLMo to process a new corpus integrating web-scale text from the mC4 and OSCAR collections, alongside aligned image-text pairs from LAION-5B and video-text data from WebVid. The core technical challenge involves developing and open-sourcing scalable, language-agnostic filtering classifiers (e.g., based on FastText or SentencePiece) to deduplicate and ensure quality across modalities and languages. We will implement a transparent data composition tool, similar to the 'AboutMe' methodology, to document the provenance and effects of each filtering decision.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) the M3D dataset itself, released under open licenses with full documentation of its creation pipeline, 2) novel, scalable filtering tools for multilingual and multimodal data, and 3) a comprehensive analysis of the dataset's characteristics and its impact on model performance across languages and modalities, providing a new open baseline for the research community.
proposals.novelty: While prior open efforts like Dolma focused on scale and OLMo on the full training stack, M3D uniquely combines massive scale with deliberate, documented multilingual and multimodal diversity from the outset, using and extending open-source tooling to make the curation of such heterogeneous data reproducible.

proposals.title: OpenTrain: A Modular, Data-Aware Framework for Reproducible and Efficient Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: The open science of language models is hindered by fragmented and opaque training codebases, which impede reproducibility and systematic study of training dynamics. Frameworks like Megatron-DeepSpeed are complex and often treat the training corpus as a monolithic, pre-processed input, obscuring the impact of data decisions documented in projects like Dolma and AboutMe. A unified, data-transparent training framework is needed to accelerate research into how data composition and training algorithms interact.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular, data-first training framework that explicitly integrates corpus metadata and filtering logic will enable more controlled experiments, improve training efficiency through better data scheduling, and yield models whose behaviors are more traceable to specific data subsets.
proposals.method: We will develop OpenTrain, an open-source PyTorch-based framework built on the OLMo codebase. Its core innovation is a `DataPipeline` API that ingests token indices alongside metadata tags (e.g., domain, quality score, filter status) from curated corpora like Dolma. We will implement and benchmark novel data scheduling algorithms (e.g., curriculum learning based on metadata, dynamic batch construction for domain balancing) and integrate them with standard optimizers and distributed training backends. The framework will include hooks for real-time training diagnostics and a suite of standardized evaluation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a production-grade, research-friendly toolkit that lowers the barrier to large-scale LM training experiments. It will produce new insights into the relationship between data scheduling and model performance, and establish best practices for reproducible, data-transparent model development.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing frameworks that treat data as a static stream, OpenTrain bakes corpus metadata directly into the training loop, enabling novel, data-aware optimization strategies. This tight integration of data provenance and training algorithms moves beyond the current paradigm of separate data processing and model training pipelines.

proposals.title: Beyond Scale: Engineering High-Quality, Small-Scale Corpora for Efficient Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in language model (LM) development prioritizes scaling training data to trillions of tokens, as seen in projects like Dolma. This approach is computationally expensive, environmentally costly, and risks amplifying biases and low-quality content from web-scale corpora. A systematic investigation into whether extreme data quality can substitute for scale is crucial for more sustainable, transparent, and performant AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model trained on a meticulously engineered, small-scale corpus—optimized for information density, factual accuracy, and structural coherence—can match or exceed the downstream task performance of a model trained on orders-of-magnitude larger, standard web-derived corpora, when controlling for compute budget.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel, high-quality corpus (target ~10B tokens) by rigorously filtering and synthesizing data from curated sources like textbooks, peer-reviewed papers, and high-quality code repositories. We will develop and apply novel metrics for 'information density' and 'learning signal' to guide curation. Using the open-source OLMo framework, we will train a series of models on this corpus and baselines (e.g., random subsets of Dolma) of equal token count. We will perform a comprehensive evaluation across diverse benchmarks (MMLU, BIG-bench, code generation) and analyze model internals to test generalization and data efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled, large-scale empirical evidence for the 'small data' hypothesis in modern LM pretraining. It will yield a novel, publicly released high-quality corpus and a reproducible methodology for data curation and evaluation. The findings could redefine scaling laws to incorporate data quality, guiding more efficient future model development.
proposals.novelty: While data filtering is common, this proposal radically inverts the scaling paradigm by explicitly testing quality as a direct substitute for quantity, an idea inspired by materials science but unexplored in LM research. It moves beyond simple filtering to actively engineer corpus structure and properties for optimal learning, a significant departure from current practice.

proposals.title: OLMo-Core: Isolating Coherence via Single-Source Pretraining and Sparse Generalization Bridges
proposals.motivation: Current large language models are trained on massive, heterogeneous corpora like Dolma, which may compromise internal coherence and systematic reasoning. While single-domain training can produce coherent but narrow models, the field lacks a principled method to isolate and preserve this coherence while enabling robust generalization. This research addresses a core tension in language model science between broad coverage and deep, consistent understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: A model pre-trained exclusively on a single, coherent source (e.g., a scientific corpus) and then fine-tuned sparsely on a small set of targeted 'bridge' examples will maintain superior internal consistency and reasoning style compared to a mixed-source model, while achieving significantly better generalization and robustness than a purely single-source model.
proposals.method: We will first pre-train a medium-scale OLMo architecture exclusively on a curated, single-domain corpus (e.g., the full text of peer-reviewed scientific articles from arXiv). We will then construct a 'bridge' dataset of high-quality examples that map core domain concepts to related out-of-domain tasks, stylistic formats, and adversarial queries. Using parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (e.g., LoRA), we will sparsely adapt the model on this bridge set. Evaluation will compare the hybrid model against a mixed-corpus baseline and the single-source model on coherence metrics, domain-specific reasoning, and out-of-domain generalization benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, reproducible framework for disentangling the effects of training data coherence from breadth. It will yield new insights into how generalization emerges from sparse interventions on a coherent base, contributing to more controlled and interpretable language model development. All code, data recipes, and model checkpoints will be released openly to accelerate the science of language models.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and multi-task training are established, the explicit isolation of a 'coherent core' followed by sparse, targeted bridging is a novel architectural hypothesis for model training. This approach directly tests whether coherence is a fragile property eroded by mixed pretraining, a question not systematically explored in prior open model efforts like OLMo and Dolma.

proposals.title: Coherence-Ablation: A Causal Framework for Disentangling Data Quality and Diversity in Language Model Training
proposals.motivation: Current large language model (LLM) training relies on massive, heterogeneous datasets like Dolma, but the causal effects of specific data attributes remain opaque. While work like AboutMe examines filter effects and OLMo promotes open science, we lack principled methods to isolate whether model capabilities stem from high-quality data sequences or the interaction of diverse sources. This hinders efficient, targeted dataset design for desired model behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically ablating quantifiable quality dimensions (e.g., logical consistency, explanatory depth) within a diverse corpus will reveal that specific model capabilities, such as reasoning coherence, are causally attributable to the presence of those quality dimensions rather than to source diversity alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the OLMo training framework to conduct controlled experiments. First, we will develop automated metrics (e.g., using entailment models, discourse parsers) to score documents in the Dolma corpus along predefined quality dimensions. We will then create training sequences where these dimensions are selectively removed or preserved via filtering, contrasting them with models trained on curated single-source corpora (e.g., textbooks for explanatory depth). We will train medium-scale OLMo models on these ablated sequences and evaluate performance on targeted benchmarks (e.g., logical deduction, explanation generation) to establish causal links.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source toolkit for causal dataset ablation, moving beyond coarse category removal. It will deliver a principled recipe linking quantifiable data attributes to model capabilities, directly informing the design of more efficient and effective training corpora for the open LLM research community.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior ablation studies that remove broad data categories, this framework merges fine-grained, quantifiable quality scoring with controlled single-source experiments to disentangle the confounded effects of quality and diversity.

proposals.title: Minimalist Filtering: Quantifying and Ablating Extreme-Risk Subsets in Web Corpora for Safer Open Language Model Training
proposals.motivation: Current large language model (LLM) pretraining relies on heavily filtered web corpora, like those used for models such as OLMo, to mitigate safety and legal risks. However, this aggressive filtering may discard vast amounts of data beneficial for model robustness and capability, a trade-off not yet rigorously quantified. A principled, evidence-based approach is needed to isolate only the most hazardous data, preserving utility while addressing critical failure modes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the most severe risks in web-scale pretraining corpora are concentrated in small, statistically identifiable subsets, and that removing only these subsets—while retaining the majority of 'noisy' data—can mitigate extreme downstream harms without significantly degrading general model capability or robustness.
proposals.method: Building on the Dolma toolkit and OLMo framework, we will first develop classifiers to identify extreme-risk subsets (e.g., violent content, unredacted PII, CSAM proxies) within a multi-trillion token raw web corpus. We will then create a series of progressively filtered training datasets, starting with only the removal of these high-confidence hazardous subsets. Using the reproducible OLMo training stack, we will train 7B-parameter comparative models on these datasets and evaluate them on standard benchmarks, targeted adversarial probes for safety and memorization, and out-of-domain robustness tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a publicly released audit suite and risk metrics for raw web corpora, 2) empirical evidence on the performance-safety trade-off curve of minimalist vs. aggressive filtering, and 3) a minimally invasive filtering recipe for open corpus development that prioritizes data utility while defensibly addressing extreme risks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that documents filter effects (AboutMe) or releases filtered corpora (Dolma), this proposal actively tests the necessity of broad filtering by performing a controlled ablation of only the most hazardous data, providing a direct causal measurement of risk concentration in web text.

proposals.title: Hygiene Hypothesis for Language Models: Controlled Exposure to Noisy Text for Improved Robustness and Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current language model pretraining relies on aggressively filtered corpora (e.g., C4, Dolma) to remove 'noise' like grammatical errors, code-switching, and informal text. While this yields clean models, it may strip away linguistic diversity and real-world complexity, potentially leading to brittle performance on adversarial or out-of-distribution tasks. This project investigates whether this filtering paradigm is optimal for building robust and generalizable models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum-based, controlled exposure to progressively noisier web text during pretraining will produce language models that are more robust to adversarial inputs and generalize better to diverse, real-world tasks, analogous to the immunological benefits of the Hygiene Hypothesis.
proposals.method: We will use the open OLMo framework and the Dolma corpus to create training datasets with graduated 'noise' levels, defined by metrics like perplexity deviation from a base LM, syntactic non-standardness, and lexical diversity. We will pretrain a series of OLMo-1B models from scratch, comparing a standard filtered baseline against models trained on curricula of increasing noise. Evaluation will extend beyond standard benchmarks to include stress tests for robustness (e.g., adversarial GLUE, ANLI), dialectal understanding, and creative generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for or against the necessity of aggressive text filtering in LLM pretraining. It will contribute a novel, controlled methodology for studying data quality's effects and release new, well-documented noisy training datasets and model checkpoints to advance reproducible research in data-centric AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., AboutMe) documents filter effects, this proposal actively tests a counter-hypothesis—that controlled noise is beneficial—by adapting a curriculum learning framework from machine learning to a novel, immunology-inspired analogy for model training.

proposals.title: DynaCorpus: A Framework and Analysis for Dynamic, Continuously Updated Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Current large language models are trained on static, snapshot-based corpora (e.g., Dolma), which rapidly become outdated and cannot incorporate new information or correct errors without costly full retraining. This limits their utility in dynamic real-world applications and hinders the scientific study of how evolving data affects model capabilities and biases over time. A shift towards dynamic training is a necessary step for more adaptive and maintainable models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that models trained on a continuously updated, versioned corpus will achieve better performance on tasks requiring recent knowledge and exhibit more stable improvement trajectories with efficient incremental updates, compared to models trained on static snapshots of equivalent total size.
proposals.method: We will extend the Dolma/OLMo open-source toolkit to create a dynamic data pipeline that ingests, processes, and versions new web-scale data monthly. We will train medium-scale OLMo architecture models using a mixture of continual pretraining (e.g., using efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA) and periodic full pretraining from different corpus snapshots. We will rigorously evaluate these models against static-baseline OLMo models on a novel temporal benchmark suite measuring factual recency, reasoning on new events, and resistance to temporal degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source framework for dynamic corpus management and continual LM pretraining at scale, alongside a public temporal evaluation benchmark. It will yield empirical evidence on the trade-offs between update strategies, offering concrete guidance for building more current and sustainable open language models.
proposals.novelty: While continual learning is studied in narrow contexts, this proposal systematically investigates open, web-scale dynamic pretraining—a paradigm shift from the static snapshot approach that underpins nearly all pre-2026 LLMs. It uniquely combines open data (Dolma), open models (OLMo), and reproducibility to establish a new methodology for LM data curation.

proposals.title: ALIGNED: Building Intrinsically Cross-Lingual Corpora for Foundational Language Model Research
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale open corpora like Dolma are predominantly English-centric, while multilingual efforts often aggregate disparate monolingual sources, creating an 'isolation risk' for underrepresented languages. This makes systematic research on cross-lingual transfer, translation, and equitable model performance difficult, as models lack aligned data to learn semantic correspondences directly. A foundational shift is needed to build corpora where cross-lingual alignment is a core design principle from the start.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing open, web-scale pretraining corpora where documents in underrepresented languages are intrinsically paired with parallel or comparable English documents across core knowledge domains, we can significantly improve the cross-lingual capabilities and evaluation of language models trained on this data compared to models trained on aggregated, unaligned multilingual data.
proposals.method: We will extend the pipeline from Dolma and OLMo to systematically gather and process web data for a set of 10-15 underrepresented languages. For each language, we will implement a coordinated crawl targeting domains like news, encyclopedic content, and instructional websites, using document structure and URL patterns to identify parallel or comparable pages with English. We will develop and open-source alignment heuristics (e.g., based on document structure, publication dates, and shared multimedia) and create a new metadata schema to tag aligned document pairs and clusters. The resulting corpus will be released with the full data provenance and processing code, following the reproducibility standards of the OLMo framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first large-scale, open multilingual corpus built with intrinsic cross-lingual alignment, enabling direct research on transfer learning and model localization. We will provide new benchmarks and analysis tools for measuring cross-lingual consistency in models like OLMo, advancing the science of language models beyond English. The released corpus, code, and documentation will serve as a foundational resource for the global NLP community.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior multilingual corpora that are post-hoc aggregations, this project embeds cross-lingual alignment as a first-class constraint in the data collection pipeline. It moves beyond simply including more languages to creating a structured, research-ready dataset where the relationship between languages is explicit and documented.

proposals.title: Linguistic Keystone Species: A Strategy for Efficient and Equitable Multilingual Corpus Development
proposals.motivation: Open language model research, exemplified by projects like OLMo and Dolma, is dominated by English-centric corpora, marginalizing thousands of languages. This creates a brittle, homogenous research ecosystem and limits the scientific understanding of language. A strategic, resource-efficient method is needed to catalyze high-quality, open resources for a more diverse set of languages.
proposals.hypothesis: Prioritizing the creation of rich, open foundational corpora for a select few 'linguistic keystone' languages—chosen for their structural similarity and resource connectivity to many other languages—will enable more efficient and higher-quality downstream corpus development for entire language families compared to a scatter-shot or English-first approach.
proposals.method: We will operationalize the keystone concept by defining selection metrics (e.g., phylogenetic centrality, speaker count, existing digital resource overlap) to nominate candidate languages (e.g., Swahili, Indonesian, Arabic). For each, we will build a comprehensive, Dolma-like open corpus, rigorously documenting data sources and filters using methods from AboutMe. We will then develop and open-source transfer pipelines (e.g., for crawling, filtering, deduplication) specifically adapted from the keystone to related target languages, measuring the efficiency and quality gains against baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide 1) a novel, ecology-inspired framework for prioritizing multilingual NLP resource development, 2) new, high-quality open corpora for initial keystone languages, and 3) reusable, documented pipelines to accelerate research into underrepresented language families, directly advancing the science of language models as called for in OLMo.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond simply advocating for more languages by providing a data-driven, efficiency-focused strategy for corpus creation, adapting the conservation biology concept of keystone species—a non-obvious analogy—to the problem of resource allocation in open NLP research.

proposals.title: Calibrating Confidence in Low-Resource LMs: A Data-Centric Approach to Reliable Early Exit
proposals.motivation: The push for open, reproducible language model science, exemplified by efforts like OLMo and Dolma, has primarily focused on high-resource languages. For low-resource languages, models trained on small, noisy, or domain-skewed corpora suffer from poorly calibrated confidence estimates. This makes computationally efficient techniques like early exit unreliable, as models cannot accurately signal when to trust their predictions, hindering equitable and efficient deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling and mitigating the calibration error introduced by low-resource training data characteristics (e.g., noise, domain shift, sparsity), we can develop early-exit mechanisms that provide reliable confidence estimates, enabling both computational efficiency and trustworthy predictions for underrepresented languages.
proposals.method: We will extend the OLMo architecture with internal classifier heads for early exit. Our core method involves a two-stage calibration process: first, a data-centric stage using techniques inspired by AboutMe to analyze and categorize corpus artifacts (e.g., web-crawl noise, domain over-representation) and their impact on per-layer confidence; second, a model-centric stage employing temperature scaling and label smoothing, but with parameters dynamically adjusted based on the data characteristics identified in stage one. We will train and evaluate on a suite of low-resource language corpora, benchmarking against standard calibration metrics (ECE, AUROC) and efficiency gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, data-aware calibration framework for early-exit LMs tailored to low-resource scenarios, 2) a reproducible analysis of how specific corpus properties affect model confidence calibration, and 3) open, efficient model checkpoints for selected low-resource languages, accompanied by full training data and code to advance transparent research in this area.
proposals.novelty: While early exit and calibration are established fields, their intersection is under-explored, especially for low-resource languages. Our approach is novel in directly linking data corpus documentation (a la AboutMe/Dolma) to the design of the calibration mechanism, moving beyond post-hoc fixes to bake in reliability from the data up.

proposals.title: Prioritizing the Underrepresented: A Framework and Corpus for Open, Non-English-First Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: The open language model (LM) research ecosystem, exemplified by projects like OLMo and Dolma, is overwhelmingly English-centric. This default reinforces a global AI inequity, limiting the benefits and scientific understanding of LMs to a fraction of the world's languages and speakers. Building foundational open resources for underrepresented languages first is a critical, unmet step toward truly inclusive and generalizable language science.
proposals.hypothesis: By prioritizing the creation of large-scale, meticulously documented pretraining corpora for specific underrepresented language families (e.g., Dravidian or Niger-Congo), we can train OLMo-style models that achieve better perplexity and downstream task performance for those languages without compromising English capabilities, challenging the necessity of English-first pretraining pipelines.
proposals.method: We will select 2-3 underrepresented language families based on speaker population and digital resource scarcity. Using the data curation pipeline and tools from Dolma and AboutMe, we will build a new open corpus, 'Dolma-UL', scaling to ~500B tokens per language family, applying language-specific filtering (e.g., fastText for language ID) and documenting data composition via cards. We will then pretrain 7B-parameter OLMo architectures from scratch on: 1) a monolingual (target language) corpus, 2) a bilingual (target + English) corpus with controlled mixing ratios, and 3) an English-only baseline. Evaluation will use existing multilingual benchmarks (e.g., AmericasNLI, XNLI) and novel language-specific tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) the first major open pretraining corpora for selected underrepresented languages, with full provenance documentation, 2) a set of open, reproducible OLMo models trained on non-English-first data, and 3) a comparative analysis of training dynamics and model capabilities under different language-priority strategies, providing a blueprint for future open, multilingual LM research.
proposals.novelty: While multilingual corpora exist, this proposal inverts the standard approach by prioritizing underrepresented languages as the primary pretraining target from the outset, rather than adding them to an English-dominated mix. It rigorously tests whether the 'English-default' in open model training is a technical necessity or a path-dependent convention.

proposals.title: The Curation Microscope: A Canonical Corpus for Controlled Experiments on Pretraining Data Effects
proposals.motivation: Large-scale language model pretraining relies on massive, often opaque data curation pipelines involving filtering, deduplication, and mixing. The causal impact of specific curation decisions on final model capabilities, biases, and safety is poorly understood, as observed in studies like AboutMe and Dolma. This lack of a controlled experimental framework makes data curation an art rather than a predictive science, hindering reproducibility and informed decision-making for projects like OLMo.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing a small, fully-documented canonical pretraining corpus and systematically creating curated variants, we can establish direct, causal links between discrete data curation operations (e.g., quality filtering thresholds, toxicity removal, domain mixing ratios) and specific, measurable outcomes in models trained from scratch on these variants.
proposals.method: We will first construct the 'Curation Microscope Corpus' (CMC), a ~10M document subset from established pre-2026 sources (e.g., C4, The Pile, Dolma samples), where each document is tagged with provenance, estimated quality (using classifiers like Gopher/CCNet filters), domain, and toxicity scores (using Perspective API or similar). Using this base, we will algorithmically generate variant corpora by applying different rule-based filtering and mixing functions. We will then repeatedly pretrain small, fixed-architecture transformer models (e.g., 124M-355M parameters, OLMo-style) from scratch on each variant and evaluate them on a consolidated benchmark covering capabilities (LM evaluation harness), social biases (CrowS-Pairs, SEAT), and safety (toxic generation prompts).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled experimental testbed for studying data curation effects, moving from correlation to causation. It will produce a public canonical corpus and a suite of pretrained model checkpoints that serve as a community resource for data ablation studies. The results will yield concrete, actionable insights into how curation decisions propagate to model behavior, directly informing the design of future large-scale corpora.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents corpus contents (Dolma) or analyzes filter effects retrospectively (AboutMe), this proposal introduces a forward-looking, *experimental* methodology that isolates curation as an independent variable. The core novelty is the creation of a miniature, instrumented 'model organism' corpus specifically designed for high-throughput, causal experimentation on data impact, a paradigm shift from observational analysis.

proposals.title: Structuring Knowledge: An Expert-Guided Framework for Curating Semantically-Organized Pretraining Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale pretraining corpora, like Dolma, prioritize scale and basic quality filtering but lack semantic structure, which is critical for high-stakes domains like law and medicine. Models trained on unstructured text struggle with complex reasoning that depends on understanding authoritative hierarchies, evidence chains, and formal ontologies. This gap limits the reliability and trustworthiness of language models in specialized, evidence-based fields.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constraining corpus curation with formal, expert-defined semantic structures (e.g., ontologies, citation graphs) will yield pretraining data that produces language models with improved performance on domain-specific reasoning tasks requiring an understanding of precedence, authority, and evidence.
proposals.method: We will extend the open-source curation toolkit from the Dolma and OLMo projects to ingest expert blueprints (e.g., legal citation networks, medical evidence hierarchies). The system will use these structures as a retrieval and scoring guide, employing graph-aware algorithms (e.g., Personalized PageRank) to prioritize documents and passages that populate and reinforce the defined relationships. The final corpus will be a multi-layer dataset including raw text, entity/concept annotations, and relational metadata, suitable for training models like OLMo with structured attention or auxiliary prediction tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, open-source corpus curation framework that integrates expert knowledge graphs, 2) a publicly released, semantically-structured pretraining corpus for a high-stakes domain (e.g., U.S. case law), and 3) an evaluation of OLMo-style models trained on this data, documenting gains in structured reasoning over baseline models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., AboutMe) studies data filters and Dolma provides scale, our method uniquely uses formal domain semantics to actively guide and structure data selection, moving beyond post-hoc analysis to proactive, knowledge-driven curation.

proposals.title: DocTriage: A Multi-Criteria Framework for Prioritizing Domain-Specific Documents in Corpus Curation
proposals.motivation: The construction of high-quality, domain-specific pretraining corpora (e.g., for law, medicine) is resource-intensive and lacks principled, automated methods for selecting documents from vast candidate pools. Current curation, as seen in projects like Dolma, often relies on broad filters, risking the omission of high-value, niche texts. A systematic framework is needed to maximize the informational utility of a corpus under practical constraints like licensing, processing cost, and data quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting structured triage protocols from conservation biology to document scoring will enable the automated creation of domain corpora that yield higher-performing language models for specialized tasks, compared to corpora curated with standard heuristics, when controlling for total token budget.
proposals.method: We will extend the open-source curation toolkit (e.g., from the OLMo/Dolma ecosystem) with a Document Triage module. This module will implement a scoring framework with four axes: 1) Informational Value (via embedding-based novelty detection and concept density metrics), 2) Quality/Reliability (via source authority classifiers and citation graph analysis), 3) Feasibility (via license detection and text complexity estimators), and 4) Functional Need (via coverage of a target domain's knowledge graph). We will develop a weighted scoring algorithm, inspired by Species Recovery Potential frameworks, to aggregate these signals and prioritize documents. The method will be validated by curating legal and biomedical corpora and pretraining/testing OLMo-style models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, open-source tool for reproducible, data-driven corpus curation and provide the first rigorous evaluation of ecological triage analogies for data selection. It will advance the science of language model pretraining by offering a principled method to build specialized corpora that balance quality, coverage, and cost.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., AboutMe, Dolma) documents data filters and sources, this proposal is the first to formally adapt a structured prioritization framework from an external discipline (conservation biology) to automate high-stakes decisions in corpus construction, moving beyond simple binary filtering.

proposals.title: OLMo-Exit: Dynamic Layer Routing for Efficient Domain Adaptation via Specialized Corpora
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are computationally expensive to run, especially when applied across diverse professional domains like law and medicine. While early-exit architectures and domain-adaptive pretraining exist, they are typically developed in isolation: generic early-exit models lack domain-specific routing intelligence, and domain-tuned models lack runtime efficiency. A synergistic approach is needed to enable expert-level accuracy with minimal compute for routine domain tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an open language model (OLMo) on a high-quality, domain-specific corpus (curated with tools from the Dolma toolkit) will enable the model to learn specialized, dynamic early-exit patterns that correlate with the linguistic and conceptual complexity of in-domain inputs, achieving superior efficiency-accuracy trade-offs compared to generic models.
proposals.method: We will use the Dolma data curation pipeline to construct high-quality corpora for two distinct domains (e.g., legal opinions and biomedical literature). We will then train a medium-scale OLMo architecture (e.g., 7B parameters) on each corpus. Crucially, we will implement a dynamic early-exit framework where every layer hosts an exit classifier, and a lightweight routing controller—trained via multi-task learning—determines whether to exit, skip to a non-adjacent layer, or continue, based on the aggregated intermediate representations. The routing policy will be jointly optimized with the language modeling objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) new open, domain-specialized pretraining corpora and models, 2) a novel dynamic layer-skipping exit architecture that learns non-sequential pathways, and 3) a comprehensive analysis of the relationship between corpus characteristics, model internals, and efficient inference, advancing the science of efficient, domain-adaptive LLMs.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop synergy: using domain-specific pretraining data not just for content adaptation, but explicitly to teach a model *how* to route itself efficiently within that domain, enabling complex, non-adjacent layer skipping patterns unseen in prior static or confidence-based early-exit work.

proposals.title: DOLMo: Domain-Specific Corpus Curation for Enhanced Legal and Medical Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: While large, general-purpose pretraining corpora like Dolma have advanced the science of language models, their broad composition dilutes domain-specific knowledge and linguistic nuance critical for fields like law and medicine. The development of high-quality, specialized models for these domains is hampered by a lack of open, reproducible, and methodologically transparent curation pipelines for creating domain-specific pretraining data at scale. This project addresses the gap by applying and extending the open-sourced toolkit from the Dolma and OLMo projects to systematically build and evaluate domain corpora.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the principled, open-source data curation toolkit developed for Dolma to specialized domains will yield pretraining corpora that, when used to train or continue training models like OLMo, produce language models with superior performance on domain-specific tasks compared to models trained solely on general web data, without degrading general capabilities.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing Dolma curation pipeline, which includes tools for filtering, deduplication, and quality tagging, with domain-specific heuristics. For law, we will source from public case law repositories (e.g., CourtListener), legislation, and legal journals; for medicine, from PubMed Central, clinical guidelines, and textbooks. We will implement and evaluate specialized filtering classifiers (e.g., to retain precise legal terminology or complex biomedical reasoning) and novel contamination checks to ensure evaluation integrity. The resulting corpora will be used to pretrain 7B-parameter OLMo architecture models from scratch and via continued pretraining, with evaluations on benchmarks like LegalBench and PubMedQA.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) two new open, high-quality, and reproducible pretraining corpora for law and medicine, documented with the 'data sheets' paradigm; 2) an extended open-source curation toolkit with domain-adaptable components; and 3) a rigorous analysis of how domain-specific pretraining data affects model capabilities, providing a blueprint for scientific corpus creation in other specialized fields.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond creating general web corpora to systematically engineer domain-specific ones using a proven, open toolkit, and it explicitly tests the impact of such curation on model performance through controlled, reproducible experiments aligned with the OLMo framework's goals for open science.

proposals.title: ProbeSign: Auditing Data Provenance in Language Models via Signature Detection and Statistical Hypothesis Testing
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) like OLMo are trained on massive, web-scale corpora like Dolma, verifying the influence of specific data subsets (e.g., copyrighted books, demographic groups) is critical for compliance, ethics, and transparency. Current influence and attribution methods are computationally intractable for trillion-token corpora and provide no framework for legally or ethically actionable claims about a model's provenance. There is a pressing need for scalable, hypothesis-driven auditing tools that can test for the detectable influence of pre-identified data sources.
proposals.hypothesis: Compact, feature-based signatures of specific data subsets can be learned by small probe models and detected within the internal activations and output distributions of a large, frozen LLM, enabling statistically rigorous tests for data influence.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where, for a target data subset (e.g., a specific copyrighted corpus), we generate a signature by training a lightweight diagnostic classifier (a 'probe') to distinguish its examples from a held-out background corpus. This probe is then applied to the intermediate layer activations or output logits of the audited LLM (e.g., OLMo) when processing new inputs. We will design a statistical hypothesis test (e.g., based on permutation testing or confidence intervals) to determine if the probe's detection rate on the LLM's behavior is significantly higher than a null distribution, indicating the subset's influence. The method will be validated using controlled experiments on the Pile and Dolma corpora.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, open-source auditing framework for data provenance, moving beyond theoretical influence measures. It will contribute a new methodology for reproducible, hypothesis-driven compliance checking in model governance. The resulting tools and statistical protocols will directly support the science of language models by enabling systematic studies of training data effects.
proposals.novelty: While probing is used for analyzing model representations, applying it as a tool for scalable, statistical *data* auditing of LLMs, framed as a hypothesis test for specific subsets, is novel. This shifts the focus from exact per-example attribution—shown to be fragile—to a practical, legally-relevant test of detectable influence.

proposals.title: Provenance-Aware Early-Exit Language Models: Joint Training for Efficiency and Data Attribution
proposals.motivation: The push for open language models (e.g., OLMo, Dolma) has intensified the need to understand how specific subsets of training data influence model capabilities and predictions. Concurrently, early-exit architectures offer computational efficiency but treat exits as generic difficulty filters. There is no framework to link an exit's specialization to the provenance of the data that shaped it, limiting both interpretability and optimal exit selection.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly training a backbone language model with its early-exit classifiers while optimizing for end-to-end data attribution, we can create a system where each exit specializes for data with distinct provenance characteristics, enabling more trustworthy and explainable dynamic inference.
proposals.method: We will extend the OLMo architecture with intermediate classifiers. During training, we will employ an influence function-based or TracIn-like loss that encourages each exit's predictions to be more attributable to a distinct, predefined subset of the training corpus (e.g., high-quality web text, academic papers, code from Dolma). This involves a multi-task objective combining standard language modeling loss with a provenance attribution loss that maximizes the alignment between an exit's gradient updates and its assigned data subset. The inference mechanism will compute a provenance similarity score for the input relative to each exit's attributed data to guide exit selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of efficient, provenance-aware LMs and an open framework for training them. It contributes a methodology for directly embedding data lineage into model architecture, advancing the science of data documentation (e.g., extending AboutMe's goals) and reproducible model analysis.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop, joint optimization of early-exit specialization and end-to-end data attribution, moving beyond post-hoc influence analysis to architecturally bake provenance sensitivity into the model's decision pathways.

proposals.title: Provenance Graphs for Pretraining: Mapping Influence and Derivation in Trillion-Token Corpora
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models (LLMs) are trained on massive, heterogeneous corpora like Dolma, where explicit citations and provenance are largely absent. This opacity makes it difficult to audit data quality, understand model biases, or document the effects of data filters, as explored in work like AboutMe. A systematic method to infer data lineage is crucial for the science of language models, reproducibility, and responsible AI development.
