proposals.hypothesis: By jointly analyzing semantic, temporal, and structural signals across web-scale pretraining data, we can construct a probabilistic provenance graph that accurately models the flow of information and derivation between documents, even in the absence of formal citations.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline for the OLMo framework that processes document pairs from corpora like Dolma. We will use dense retrieval models (e.g., Contriever) for semantic similarity, named entity recognition and coreference for factual overlap, and publication timestamps (when available) for temporal precedence. For structural replication, we will analyze HTML DOM trees and n-gram patterns. These signals will be integrated via a learned classifier (e.g., XGBoost) to output edge weights in a directed provenance graph, which we will validate against known citation networks and synthetic derivatives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source tool for large-scale data provenance mapping within LLM pretraining corpora, directly advancing the science of language models. It will enable new analyses of filter effects, contamination between training and evaluation data, and the ecological dynamics of information in web-scale datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tracks dataset ingredients (e.g., AboutMe) or builds corpora (e.g., Dolma), this proposal focuses on inferring fine-grained, document-level influence relationships *within* these corpora using a multi-modal signal fusion approach, moving beyond simple duplication detection.

proposals.title: Discovering the Unknown Domain: An Active Learning Framework for Assessing and Improving Corpus Coverage
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale pretraining corpora, like Dolma, are constructed by filtering and sampling from massive web crawls, treating the target domain (e.g., 'high-quality English text') as a known population. This approach risks systematic gaps and biases, as the domain's true conceptual structure is assumed, not discovered. A method to actively discover and quantify coverage of the latent domain is crucial for building more representative, transparent, and scientifically grounded language models like OLMo.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a target domain as a latent structure to be discovered via iterative, adaptive sampling, we can produce a more accurate and quantifiable assessment of corpus coverage than static matching methods, explicitly identifying underrepresented conceptual strata.
proposals.method: We will develop an active learning system that first uses topic modeling (e.g., BERTopic) and clustering on an initial seed corpus to induce a preliminary model of domain strata. An adaptive sampler, using uncertainty or diversity sampling strategies, will then query external APIs (e.g., Common Crawl, arXiv, Project Gutenberg) and structured sources to solicit documents that test the boundaries of these clusters. The process iterates, refining the domain model via online clustering and providing a coverage score per stratum. Gaps are documented as low-density regions in the discovered conceptual space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel framework and open-source tool for corpus builders to diagnose coverage gaps proactively, 2) a quantifiable, data-driven measure of domain coverage to complement heuristic filters, and 3) explicit documentation of uncovered domain regions, advancing reproducibility and transparency in dataset creation as championed in the AboutMe and Dolma papers.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard corpus creation paradigm from filtering a known universe to actively discovering an unknown domain structure. It integrates active learning for domain discovery—a technique common in small-data settings—into the massive-scale, open corpus research context.

proposals.title: Modeling the Information Niche: A Framework for Quantifying and Improving Corpus Representativeness
proposals.motivation: Large, open corpora like Dolma are foundational for language model science, but their representativeness is assessed with simplistic heuristics like word frequency. This fails to capture complex linguistic and topical dimensions, risking systematic gaps that bias model capabilities and evaluations. A principled, quantitative method is needed to audit and guide corpus construction, ensuring models are trained on data that truly reflects target domains.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting Environmental Niche Modeling (ENM) techniques from ecology, we can model a corpus's coverage of a multidimensional linguistic feature space, identify underrepresented regions, and generate targeted sampling strategies that improve representativeness more effectively than size-based scaling alone.
proposals.method: We will define a feature space using dimensions from pre-2026 NLP: topic distributions (via BERTopic), syntactic complexity metrics, and register features (e.g., from the Text Interchange Formats schema). For a target domain (e.g., scientific text), we will build a reference distribution from metadata or a broad web crawl. Using the MaxEnt algorithm (a standard ENM method), we will model the probability of corpus 'occurrence' across this feature space. Underrepresented niches are identified as high-probability regions in the reference with low sampling density. We will then implement stratified sampling to collect new text targeting these gaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first operational framework for quantifying corpus representativeness beyond surface statistics. It will yield a reproducible, open-source tool for corpus audit and a case study demonstrating improved domain coverage. This directly advances the science of language models by making data curation more systematic and auditable.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the cross-disciplinary adaptation of a mature ecological modeling paradigm to the corpus analysis problem, moving from descriptive statistics to predictive modeling of coverage gaps. This approach is distinct from prior NLP work on data selection or balancing, which lacks a formal model of the unsampled space.

proposals.title: DOLMo: Domain-Only Language Model Corpora for Controlled Analysis of Data-Domain Effects
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale pretraining corpora like Dolma are mixtures of many domains, making it impossible to isolate the contribution of specific data sources to model capabilities and biases. This opacity hinders the scientific understanding of language models and the development of optimized, responsible data mixtures. A controlled, reproducible testbed is needed to move from correlation to causation in data-centric AI research.
proposals.hypothesis: By pretraining models on small, canonical, and perfectly documented single-domain corpora, we can establish causal links between specific domain data and the emergence of measurable skills, biases, and representational structures in language models.
proposals.method: We will extend the open-source toolkit from Dolma and OLMo to curate a suite of ~10B-token corpora, each representing a distinct domain (e.g., law, biomedicine, code, news). Each corpus will be created using reproducible pipelines with exhaustive metadata, following the documentation principles of AboutMe. We will then conduct full-scale pretraining of OLMo-style architectures from scratch on each corpus and on controlled mixtures, analyzing resulting models on domain-specific benchmarks, probing tasks, and bias evaluations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a public suite of benchmark corpora and associated model checkpoints, creating a shared experimental platform for the community. It will deliver foundational insights into domain-data-to-capability mapping and provide a rigorous methodology for auditing and optimizing training data composition for desired model behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates large mixed corpora or studies filtered data, this proposal systematically constructs *minimal*, pure-domain corpora specifically for controlled ablation. It treats data domains as independent variables in a scientific experiment, a paradigm shift from observational analysis of existing blends.

proposals.title: CausalMed: A Toolkit for Causal Mediation Analysis of Training Data Sources in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Understanding the causal effect of specific training data sources (e.g., web domains, curated books, code) on model behaviors is critical for transparency, safety, and data curation. Current methods rely on costly and confounded retraining on ablated corpora, which fails to isolate source effects due to non-additive interactions in the training mixture. A method to analyze a fixed, pre-trained model is needed to directly attribute capabilities and biases to their data origins.
proposals.hypothesis: The causal contribution of a specific training data source to a language model's behavior can be isolated by applying causal mediation analysis to the model's internal representations, using gradient-based feature attribution and representation patching to simulate its removal.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python toolkit that, given a pre-trained model (e.g., OLMo) and metadata about its training data sources (e.g., from Dolma), implements a causal mediation framework. The core method adapts techniques from causal abstraction and interchange interventions: for a given input, we will compute the gradient-based importance of training examples from a target source (inspired by TracIn) to identify critical model components. We will then perform representation patching at intermediate layers to 'subtract' the estimated influence of that source, creating a counterfactual model state. The causal effect is measured as the difference in output distributions (e.g., logits, specific behavior probabilities) between the original and counterfactual states across a diagnostic benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This toolkit will provide the first practical, computationally efficient method for estimating the causal effect of data sources without retraining, advancing the science of data provenance. It will produce quantitative, source-specific effect estimates on model capabilities and biases, directly supporting research on data filtering (as in AboutMe) and corpus design. The tool will be open-sourced with documentation and case studies on the OLMo models to ensure reproducibility.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., TracIn, causal mediation in vision) attributes influence to individual training examples, this approach scales to entire data sources and explicitly models the counterfactual of source removal within a fixed model. It moves beyond correlational analysis of data mixtures to a causal, intervention-based framework for training data analysis.

proposals.title: Causal Data Cartography: Validating Quality Metrics via Systematic Data Ablation
proposals.motivation: The rapid scaling of pretraining corpora, exemplified by projects like Dolma, has outpaced our understanding of how specific data sources contribute to model capabilities. Current data quality metrics are largely heuristic and validated only by correlation with final model performance, failing to establish a causal link between data properties and specific downstream effects. This gap hinders the scientific development of language models and the reproducibility of training outcomes, core challenges highlighted by the OLMo initiative.
proposals.hypothesis: The causal impact of a data subset on model performance, measured via controlled ablation studies, provides the necessary ground truth for rigorously validating and calibrating data quality metrics. A metric's true utility is its predictive power for which data removals will most affect specific model capabilities.
proposals.method: We will construct a series of controlled training experiments using the OLMo framework, ablating specific, documented sources from the Dolma corpus. For each ablated variant, we will train a 1B-parameter model and evaluate performance differentials across a benchmark suite. We will then compute a suite of candidate quality metrics (e.g., deduplication scores, perplexity-based filters, domain classifiers) on the ablated data slices. Finally, we will use causal inference methods, such as propensity score matching on corpus metadata and regression discontinuity designs, to test how well each metric predicts the observed performance changes from ablation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first framework for causally validating data quality metrics, moving the field beyond correlative proxies. We will release a public benchmark of ablation-based performance differentials linked to Dolma subsets, enabling future metric development. The resulting 'causally-grounded' metrics will directly inform more effective and interpretable data curation strategies for open language model science.
proposals.novelty: While data ablation studies exist, they have not been systematically used as ground truth for metric validation. Framing metric utility as a causal prediction problem, rather than a correlative one, is a novel and necessary shift for advancing data-centric language model research.

proposals.title: Beyond BERT: A Systematic Audit of Data Filtering Effects Across Pretrained Language Model Architectures
proposals.motivation: Recent work, such as AboutMe, has demonstrated that English pretraining data filters can systematically bias models by disproportionately removing content from certain demographic groups. However, these findings are primarily based on BERT-family models (e.g., OLMo). It is unknown whether these effects generalize to other influential pretraining paradigms like RoBERTa's dynamic masking or XLNet's permutation-based objective, which may interact with data composition differently. Understanding these architectural interactions is crucial for diagnosing and mitigating biases in the broader ecosystem of open language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the documented demographic biases induced by pretraining data filters are not uniform but are instead modulated by core architectural choices and pretraining objectives. Specifically, we predict that models trained with different objectives on the same filtered corpus (e.g., Dolma) will exhibit measurable differences in the representation and performance on content associated with filtered demographic groups.
proposals.method: We will apply the experimental methodology from AboutMe to RoBERTa, XLNet, and a BERT baseline, all pretrained from scratch on controlled subsets of the Dolma corpus. Using the same template-based probing and intrinsic evaluation tasks, we will measure model performance on self-descriptive text associated with various demographics. The analysis will correlate performance gaps with architectural variables (e.g., attention pattern, training objective) and the documented filtering metadata from the pretraining data, using statistical models to isolate the effect of architecture from data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comparative audit of data filter effects across major LM architectures, moving beyond a single-model understanding. It will produce actionable insights for the open science community on how architectural decisions interact with data curation to shape model biases. The resulting code and reproducible training pipelines will extend the OLMo framework, enabling future research on architecture-data interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied data filters or model architectures in isolation, systematically testing how architectural choices *mediate* the documented effects of data filtering is a novel and critical direction. This directly extends the reproducible, open science approach of OLMo and Dolma to a comparative analysis that has not been conducted at scale.

proposals.title: Predicting Fine-Tuning Outcomes from Pretraining Dynamics: A Data-Driven Analysis Using OLMo's Public Training Traces
proposals.motivation: The open release of training data (e.g., Dolma) and models (e.g., OLMo) has accelerated the science of language models, but the relationship between pretraining dynamics and downstream fine-tuning success remains poorly understood. As models grow larger and datasets more curated, predicting which pretrained checkpoints will excel on specific tasks is crucial for efficient resource allocation and understanding model capabilities. This problem is central to improving the transparency and reproducibility of the LM development lifecycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's fine-tuning performance on a target task can be predicted from its publicly released pretraining validation loss trajectories and per-token scores, without requiring expensive fine-tuning experiments on all checkpoints. Specifically, we posit that patterns in loss on specific data domains during pretraining are indicative of acquired capabilities transferable to related downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will use the OLMo framework's fully released training logs and validation scores across the Dolma corpus sub-components. For a suite of fine-tuning tasks (e.g., from the LM Evaluation Harness), we will establish a ground-truth performance matrix by fine-tuning a subset of OLMo checkpoints. We will then train regression models (e.g., gradient-boosted trees like XGBoost and simple neural networks) to predict final fine-tuning metrics using features derived from pretraining: validation loss curves per data source, loss variance, and learning rate schedules. We will analyze feature importance to identify which pretraining data domains are most predictive for which task families.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a predictive framework for checkpoint selection, reducing the computational cost of finding optimal pretrained bases for fine-tuning. It will contribute a novel analysis linking explicit pretraining data filters and dynamics to downstream outcomes, enhancing the interpretability of the training process. The resulting methodology and code will be released as an open tool for the community, extending the reproducibility ethos of the OLMo project.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes final model performance, predicting fine-tuning success from intermediate, per-data-source training signals is novel. This directly leverages the unprecedented openness of the OLMo project's training traces, moving beyond post-hoc analysis to proactive prediction.

proposals.title: Scaling Key-Value Memory Networks to Web Corpora: Efficient Indexing for Trillion-Token Knowledge Access
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) like OLMo are trained on massive, open corpora like Dolma, but their parametric knowledge is static after training and difficult to update. Key-Value Memory Neural Networks (KV-MemNNs) offer a promising architecture for augmenting models with a non-parametric, updatable external memory, but existing implementations are limited to toy datasets or thousands of documents. To be useful for real-world applications, these mechanisms must scale to the millions of documents present in web-scale pretraining corpora, enabling models to access and cite precise, time-sensitive information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining dense retrieval techniques (like those used in DPR or ColBERT) with a hierarchical or approximate nearest-neighbor index (e.g., FAISS), we can build a KV-MemNN system that efficiently retrieves relevant context from a corpus of millions of documents, significantly improving the factuality and attribution capabilities of a base OLMo-style model without expensive retraining.
proposals.method: We will construct a memory bank from the Dolma corpus, encoding document chunks into dense vectors using a frozen, pretrained retriever encoder. We will implement and benchmark several scalable indexing strategies, including IVF indexes in FAISS and hierarchical navigable small world graphs. The retrieval-augmented OLMo model will, for a given query, use the index to fetch the top-k document chunks, which will be passed as key-value pairs into the KV-MemNN attention layer. We will rigorously evaluate the system on open-domain QA and fact-updating tasks, measuring accuracy, latency, and memory footprint against baseline retrieval-augmented generation models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, reproducible framework for scaling KV-MemNNs to web corpora, directly advancing the science of language model architectures. It will provide empirical results on the trade-offs between retrieval accuracy, speed, and model performance at scale, offering a practical path for making large open models more factual and updatable. The resulting system will be a key tool for studying the effects of corpus composition, as in the AboutMe line of research.
proposals.novelty: While retrieval-augmented generation is an active area, the specific application and scaling of the KV-MemNN architecture—a model with a differentiable, attention-based memory—to a multi-million-document index derived from a massive open corpus like Dolma is novel. This bridges the gap between a specific, promising model architecture and the scale of data used for modern LLM pretraining.

proposals.title: Conditional Instance Complexity: Predicting Learning Difficulty from Pretraining Data Quality Priors
proposals.motivation: Current language model training treats data as a uniform stream, despite known quality variations that affect model performance. The field lacks a principled, predictive framework linking fine-grained attributes of pretraining data (e.g., from corpora like Dolma) to the difficulty a resulting model will have with new instances. This gap hinders efficient data curation, curriculum design, and model capacity allocation, which are critical for advancing open, reproducible language model science as championed by projects like OLMo.
proposals.hypothesis: The learning complexity of a data instance for a given model is not fixed but is conditional on the quality characteristics of the model's pretraining data; we can predict this conditional complexity by using novel data quality metrics as priors.
proposals.method: We will develop automated metrics for semantic density (via embedding cluster coherence), factual consistency (using knowledge graph alignment on a subset), and representational typicality (distance to high-density regions of the training manifold). Using the OLMo framework and its training data (e.g., Dolma subsets), we will train diagnostic probes to predict per-instance loss or uncertainty. We will then formulate a conditional complexity function, implemented as a lightweight neural network or Gaussian Process regressor, that maps from these quality priors to predicted learning difficulty on held-out or new task data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, operationalized framework for understanding how data quality propagates to model behavior, 2) practical tools for proactive data curation and curriculum learning, and 3) new insights for the open science of language models by documenting these relationships in a reproducible manner, extending the data documentation ethos of AboutMe.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents data filters or measures aggregate dataset quality, this proposal innovates by defining instance-specific complexity as a conditional function of pretraining data quality, enabling predictive and proactive use of quality metrics rather than retrospective analysis.

proposals.title: DynaExit: Dynamic Early Exits for Efficient and Robust Pretraining on Noisy Web Corpora
proposals.motivation: Training state-of-the-art language models requires massive, web-scale corpora like Dolma, but these datasets are inherently noisy and heterogeneous. Current methods rely on static, one-time filtering, which is computationally expensive and risks removing valuable linguistic diversity. A dynamic, in-training method to handle noise could dramatically reduce the computational burden of training on raw data while potentially improving model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating trainable early-exit classifiers into the model architecture will enable dynamic, per-example decisions during pretraining, allowing for efficient, lightweight updates on simple or noisy data and full-model updates only on high-utility examples, thereby reducing overall compute and improving early-layer feature extraction.
proposals.method: We will integrate auxiliary classifier heads at intermediate layers of a transformer model like OLMo. During each forward pass, these heads will produce confidence scores and complexity estimates for the input. A gating policy, trained via reinforcement learning or a lightweight auxiliary loss, will decide to: 1) apply a loss and update only up to that early exit (for simple/noisy data), 2) skip the backward pass entirely (for unusable noise), or 3) proceed through the full model. We will benchmark this on the Dolma corpus, using the data documentation methods from AboutMe to analyze the characteristics of routed examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, efficient training paradigm for large-scale pretraining, directly reducing FLOPs and communication costs in distributed settings. It will also provide new insights into the relationship between data quality, model architecture, and training dynamics, advancing the science of open language model development as championed by the OLMo project.
proposals.novelty: While early exits are established for efficient inference, their integration into the training loop for dynamic data routing and compute-aware gradient updates on raw, noisy pretraining data is a novel and unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Inferring Pretraining Data Recipes via Task-Agnostic Early-Exit Signatures
proposals.motivation: Understanding the composition of massive pretraining corpora, like those used for models such as OLMo, is critical for auditing model biases and capabilities. Current methods for data attribution are often task-specific or rely on direct dataset probing, which provides a fragmented view. A unified, task-agnostic framework for inferring data influence is needed to advance the science of language model training and improve reproducibility.
proposals.hypothesis: The pattern of early-exit behavior—the specific layers at which a model confidently produces correct outputs—across a diverse suite of NLP tasks forms a unique signature that can be inverted to probabilistically infer the composition and relative influence of data domains in the model's pretraining corpus.
proposals.method: We will instrument a suite of open language models (e.g., OLMo) with early-exit classifiers at each transformer layer. We will then evaluate these models on a broad, curated benchmark covering sequence labeling, QA, and generation tasks, recording per-task exit layers. Using this multi-task signature matrix, we will train an inference model (e.g., a Bayesian neural network) to map exit patterns to simulated pretraining data mixtures, validated against known-corpus models like those trained on Dolma subsets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic tool for model auditing, linking observed capabilities directly to pretraining data origins. It will also contribute a framework for more efficient architecture design by identifying which model components are specialized for which data types, advancing the goals of open and reproducible language model research.
proposals.novelty: While early-exit is studied for efficiency, its systematic use as a multi-faceted signature for the inverse problem of data recipe inference is novel. This approach moves beyond single-task probing to a unified, task-agnostic analysis of training data influence.

proposals.title: Evaluating Adaptive Early Exit for Efficient Inference Across Diverse NLP Tasks
proposals.motivation: Early exit methods, which allow models to halt computation at intermediate layers, have shown promise for accelerating inference in classification tasks. However, their efficacy and optimal design for core NLP tasks like sequence labeling, question answering, and text generation remain underexplored. A systematic evaluation is needed to understand if the efficiency gains from these methods generalize across diverse task structures, which is crucial for deploying efficient, open language models like OLMo in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adaptive early exit strategies, where the exit point is dynamically determined per token or sequence, can maintain high task performance while significantly reducing computational cost across a broad spectrum of NLP tasks, but that the optimal strategy will vary by task type.
proposals.method: We will implement and benchmark multiple early exit algorithms (e.g., PABEE, DeeBERT, CALM) within the open-source OLMo framework. Using the Dolma corpus for continued pretraining if needed, we will fine-tune and evaluate models on standardized benchmarks for named entity recognition (sequence labeling), SQuAD (question answering), and summarization (generation). We will measure the trade-off between task-specific performance (F1, EM, ROUGE) and computational savings (FLOPs, latency), analyzing how exit policies interact with task demands.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, reproducible analysis of early exit for major NLP task categories beyond classification, delivering concrete guidelines for practitioners. It will contribute open-source code, fine-tuned checkpoint variants, and detailed performance profiles to the science of language models, advancing the goal of efficient and accessible NLP.
proposals.novelty: While early exit is known for classification, its systematic application and comparison across generative and structured prediction tasks is novel. The study uniquely leverages a fully open ecosystem (OLMo, Dolma) to ensure reproducible, foundational insights, moving beyond proprietary model evaluations.

proposals.title: M3E: A Massively Multitask, Multilingual Model with Adaptive Early Exiting for Efficient Inference
proposals.motivation: The trend towards training single, massive models to handle hundreds of diverse tasks and languages (e.g., FLAN, BLOOM) creates significant computational burdens for deployment and inference. Early exiting, which allows samples to exit through intermediate layers, has shown promise for efficiency in smaller models, but its application and scaling dynamics in a truly massive, open, multitask setting remain unexplored. This research addresses the critical need for making large-scale, general-purpose models practically usable.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, giant OLMo-style model, trained on a massively multitask and multilingual curriculum using open corpora like Dolma, can be equipped with a robust, task-and-language-adaptive early exiting mechanism that maintains high performance across all tasks while dramatically reducing average inference latency and computational cost.
proposals.method: We will first train a dense decoder-only model (e.g., 70B+ parameters) on a curated mixture of the Dolma corpus and a massive multitask instruction dataset spanning hundreds of tasks and languages. We will then integrate and compare several adaptive early exit methods (e.g., PABEE, DeeBERT, CALM), training lightweight internal classifiers at select transformer layers. The key innovation is the development of an exit gate conditioned not just on internal representations but also on explicit task and language identifiers, allowing the model to learn distinct efficiency profiles per task family. We will rigorously evaluate on held-out tasks from the training distribution and novel tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a large, open, multitask/multilingual model with integrated early exiting, released with full training code and data documentation in the style of OLMo and AboutMe; 2) a comprehensive analysis of how early exiting behavior correlates with task and language complexity; 3) a reproducible framework for building efficient, general-purpose models, advancing the science of language model scaling.
proposals.novelty: While early exiting has been studied for single tasks or narrow domains, applying it adaptively within a single model serving an open-ended set of hundreds of diverse tasks and languages is a novel and significant scaling challenge. The integration of explicit task/language conditioning into the exit decision mechanism is a new technical direction for improving exit accuracy in this complex setting.

proposals.title: SciDolma: A High-Quality, Domain-Curated Corpus for Scientific Language Model Pretraining
proposals.motivation: The rapid scaling of pretraining corpora, as exemplified by projects like Dolma, has prioritized broad coverage over domain-specific quality. For scientific and educational applications, models trained on general web data often lack precision and factual reliability. Creating high-quality, curated subsets for these domains is crucial for advancing specialized language models that can power trustworthy research tools and educational assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a multi-stage, rule-based and classifier-driven curation pipeline to a large-scale open corpus like Dolma will yield a scientific subset that, when used for pretraining, produces language models with superior performance on domain-specific tasks compared to models trained on unfiltered or heuristically filtered data.
proposals.method: We will develop a transparent, reproducible curation pipeline for the Dolma corpus. The method involves: 1) Heuristic filtering using URL patterns and keyword matching from known scientific repositories (e.g., arXiv, PubMed, OSTI). 2) Training a lightweight, interpretable classifier (e.g., fastText) on a seed set of labeled scientific documents to score remaining documents. 3) Applying a conservative quality filter based on document structure and citation density. 4) Releasing the resulting SciDolma corpus with full provenance metadata, following the OLMo framework's commitment to open data and reproducibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) The SciDolma corpus, a large-scale, openly licensed, and meticulously documented scientific pretraining dataset. 2) A reproducible, open-source curation pipeline that can be adapted for other high-value domains. 3) An empirical analysis, using the OLMo training framework, comparing the performance of models trained on SciDolma versus baseline corpora on a suite of scientific reasoning benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created scientific datasets (e.g., C4, The Pile), our approach is novel in its direct application to and provenance tracking within a trillion-token-scale corpus like Dolma, and its integration with the full-stack, open-source OLMo ecosystem for controlled training experiments.

proposals.title: OLMo-Domain: A Framework for Building and Evaluating Open, Specialized Language Models from Curated Technical Corpora
proposals.motivation: While large-scale, general-purpose pretraining corpora like Dolma have advanced the science of language models, their utility for specialized technical domains (e.g., scientific literature, legal documents, or source code) remains limited due to dilution by general web text. The community lacks a reproducible, data-centric framework for constructing high-quality, domain-specific pretraining datasets and rigorously evaluating the trade-offs of focused vs. general pretraining. This gap hinders the development of efficient, expert-level AI assistants in critical fields.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that models trained on carefully curated, open domain-specific corpora, using the transparent OLMo framework, will achieve superior performance on domain-specific tasks compared to general models of similar scale, and that the performance gap can be systematically explained by the quality and concentration of domain-relevant tokens as measured by methods like those in AboutMe.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Dolma curation pipeline to create open, reproducible corpora for 2-3 technical domains (e.g., peer-reviewed CS papers and associated code). Using the OLMo training codebase, we will train a suite of 1-7B parameter models, varying the mixture of domain-specific and general data. We will rigorously evaluate these models against general OLMo checkpoints on domain-specific benchmarks (e.g., code generation, technical QA) and general NLP tasks, analyzing performance correlates with dataset composition metrics inspired by AboutMe's self-description analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a public framework and open datasets for reproducible domain-specific LM research, 2) a detailed empirical study of the scaling laws and trade-offs for focused pretraining, and 3) new, open domain-specialized OLMo model checkpoints. It will provide a blueprint for building efficient expert models without full-scale pretraining.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the full-stack open science methodology of the OLMo project—encompassing data curation, training, and evaluation—to the understudied problem of domain specialization, moving beyond simple continued pretraining to a controlled, data-centric investigation of corpus design effects.

proposals.title: StreamingDolma: A Framework for Dynamic, Continuously Updated Language Model Pretraining Corpora
proposals.motivation: Static, snapshot-based pretraining corpora like Dolma and C4 quickly become outdated, failing to capture the evolving nature of language, culture, and knowledge on the web. This temporal misalignment between training data and the real world can degrade model performance on current information and introduce subtle temporal biases. A dynamic, streaming corpus is essential for studying the effects of data recency on model capabilities and for enabling more sustainable, incremental model updates.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuously updated, streaming pretraining corpus will enable the training of language models that are more temporally robust and can be updated more efficiently than models trained on static snapshots, while also providing a novel testbed for analyzing the impact of data dynamics on model behavior.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a scalable, versioned data pipeline that continuously ingests and processes new web content from Common Crawl and other public web archives. The system will extend the Dolma toolkit to apply reproducible, incremental filtering (e.g., quality, toxicity, deduplication) and document-level versioning. We will employ a distributed streaming architecture (e.g., Apache Kafka/Flink) for data flow and a metadata schema to track provenance and temporal attributes, enabling the creation of longitudinal dataset slices for controlled experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open, large-scale framework for building and studying dynamic language model corpora, directly supporting research into temporal adaptation and efficient retraining. It will yield a public, continuously updating data stream (StreamingDolma) and a set of analysis tools to quantify the effects of data flux on model science, advancing the reproducibility and temporal validity of LM research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (Dolma, AboutMe) focuses on static corpus creation and analysis, this proposal shifts the paradigm to continuous, versioned data streams. This enables fundamentally new research questions about the lifecycle of pretraining data and models, moving beyond one-time snapshots.

ideas.title: Explore the dual: a model that identifies training data sources
ideas.description: Build a model to audit and attribute LLM outputs back to corpus sources like ROOTS.
ideas.sourceTitle: BLOOM: A 176B-Parameter Open-Access Multilingual Language Model

ideas.title: High-Quality, Small-Scale Corpora for Language Models: A 'Small Data' Approach Inspired by Materials Science
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that language models require trillion-token datasets by drawing an analogy from materials science. In that field, a small, perfectly structured crystal outperforms a large, impure bulk material. The analogous solution is focusing on atomic-level quality and structure over sheer mass. Adapted to AI, the idea is to research whether an extremely high-quality, small-scale corpus—meticulously engineered for purity, information density, and optimal learning structure—can match or surpass models trained on massive, noisy datasets. The core hypothesis is that data quality and intelligent curation can be a substitute for scale, leading to more efficient, capable, and generalizable models.

ideas.title: Investigate the inverse: what minimal data enables multilingual learning?
ideas.description: Find the smallest possible curated dataset that can bootstrap a model like BLOOM.
ideas.sourceTitle: BLOOM: A 176B-Parameter Open-Access Multilingual Language Model

ideas.title: The Curation Microscope: A Miniature Benchmark Corpus for Isolating and Amplifying Long-Term Data Curation Effects
ideas.description: This research creates a small, meticulously documented 'canonical' pretraining corpus where every document is tagged with provenance and specific curation attributes (e.g., quality score, domain, toxicity level, synthetic vs. organic). Researchers then systematically create variant corpora by applying different filtering and mixing rules to this base set. By repeatedly pretraining small models from scratch on these variants, the project establishes a direct, causal link between discrete curation decisions and resulting model capabilities, biases, and safety failures. This turns the slow, observational study of curation effects into a fast, experimental science, enabling the development of predictive theories of data impact that can inform curation for large-scale training.

ideas.title: Analyze effects of machine-generated text contamination
ideas.description: Investigate how machine-translated or other synthetic text in training data influences model capabilities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Documenting Large Webtext Corpora: A Case Study on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus

ideas.title: Expert-Guided, Semantically-Structured Corpus Curation for High-Stakes Domains
ideas.description: This system combines an open-source data curation toolkit with a structured expert-knowledge integration framework to build pretraining corpora for fields like law and medicine. Domain experts first collaborate to define formal ontologies, concept graphs, and citation/evidence hierarchies specific to the field. The curation toolkit is then constrained and directed by these expert blueprints, prioritizing documents and passages that populate and reinforce these validated semantic structures. The final output is a corpus that is not only high-quality in terms of deduplication and noise reduction but is also semantically organized to train models that understand authoritative relationships, precedence, and evidence chains within the specialized domain.

ideas.title: Extend analysis to more tasks and model types
ideas.description: Apply the validation performance vs. computation analysis to a wider range of NLP tasks and model architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Show Your Work: Improved Reporting of Experimental Results

ideas.title: Canonical Miniature Domain Corpora for Controlled Cross-Domain Pretraining Analysis
ideas.description: This project creates a suite of small, perfectly documented, and high-quality benchmark corpora, each representing a distinct specialized domain like law, medicine, and computer science, using a refined version of the open-source curation toolkit. Each 'mini-corpus' is designed to be a canonical, size-controlled sample of its domain, enabling fast, fully reproducible pretraining runs from scratch or as domain injections into a base model. This allows researchers to conduct controlled ablation studies to precisely measure how data from each domain contributes to the development of specific model skills and biases. The result is a powerful, shared experimental testbed for understanding data-domain-to-capability relationships, optimizing multi-domain mixture recipes, and benchmarking data curation methods themselves.

ideas.title: Apply toolkit to curate specialized domain corpora
ideas.description: Use the open-sourced data curation toolkit to build high-quality, domain-specific pretraining corpora for fields like law or medicine.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Generate synthetic 'web-like' corpora
ideas.description: Create synthetic training data that mimics the documented properties of web corpora but with controlled attributes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Documenting Large Webtext Corpora: A Case Study on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus

ideas.title: Applying the Hygiene Hypothesis to Model Training: Controlled Exposure to 'Noise' for Robustness
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that aggressive text filtering is necessary for training high-quality language models by drawing an analogy from immunology's Hygiene Hypothesis. In that field, controlled exposure to environmental microbes is essential for developing a robust immune system, preventing over-sensitivity. The analogous solution is managed exposure rather than complete sterility. Adapted to AI research, this proposes training models on web data with controlled, graduated levels of 'noise'—such as grammatical imperfections, diverse dialects, and unfiltered opinions—instead of heavily sanitized corpora. The core hypothesis is that this exposure, like an immune challenge, will produce models that are more robust to adversarial inputs, more creative in generation, and better at generalizing to novel or messy real-world tasks, potentially revealing that current filtering practices strip away valuable signal for adaptation and resilience.

ideas.title: Study the impact of dataset composition on model behavior
ideas.description: Analyze how different sources in the ROOTS corpus affect model outputs and biases.
ideas.sourceTitle: BLOOM: A 176B-Parameter Open-Access Multilingual Language Model

ideas.title: Domain-Adaptive Early Exiting via Specialized Corpora and Dynamic Layer Routing
ideas.description: This research direction uses the open-sourced data curation toolkit to build high-quality, domain-specific pretraining corpora (e.g., for law, medicine). These corpora are then used to train a model where all layers contribute equally to exit decisions, enabling it to learn specialized routing patterns—skipping non-adjacent layers or selecting intermediate classifiers—based on the linguistic and conceptual complexity of the input text. The synergy enables the model to achieve expert-level accuracy with minimal computational cost for straightforward domain queries, while dynamically engaging deeper reasoning for novel or complex sub-problems, a capability neither static corpora nor generic early-exit models possess alone.

ideas.title: Scale down to domain-specific corpora
ideas.description: Apply the method at a smaller scale for specialized technical domains.

ideas.title: Invert the goal from clean to representative
ideas.description: Prioritize creating a web corpus that demographically and topically represents the internet, not a 'cleaned' subset.

ideas.title: Early Exit for Efficient Training on Raw, Noisy Data
ideas.description: This research explores a novel training paradigm for large language models on 'raw,' minimally filtered web data. We propose integrating early exits directly into the model architecture and training loop, where simpler, early classifier heads dynamically assess the complexity, noise, or learning utility of each data sample during a forward pass. Based on this assessment, the system can decide to commit a lightweight update using only the early layers, skip backward passes through the full model for unusable noise, or trigger full-model processing only for high-value, complex examples. This approach synergistically reduces the computational, memory, and communication overhead of distributed training on massive, noisy datasets, while theoretically encouraging robustness by forcing early layers to develop better feature extractors for disambiguating signal from noise.

ideas.title: Scale model to web-scale document corpora

ideas.title: Confidence-Calibrated Early Exit for Low-Resource Language Models
ideas.description: This research direction focuses on developing foundational models for non-English languages that incorporate confidence-calibrated early-exit mechanisms. The core challenge is that early-exit strategies, which save computation by halting inference early when a prediction is deemed confident, are unreliable when trained on the small, noisy, or domain-skewed corpora typical for low-resource languages. We will develop novel calibration techniques specifically designed for these data-scarce, high-uncertainty scenarios, enabling models to accurately estimate prediction trustworthiness. This allows for the creation of models that are not only more accessible by prioritizing underrepresented languages but are also computationally efficient and reliably indicate when their outputs can be trusted, a critical feature for equitable deployment.

ideas.title: Test on broader range of NLP tasks
ideas.description: Evaluate the early exit method on sequence labeling, question answering, and generation tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Right Tool for the Job: Matching Model and Instance Complexities

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Assess Corpus Representativeness
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology for assessing sampling bias in species occurrence data to the problem of evaluating corpus representativeness. In ecology, Environmental Niche Modeling (ENM) compares the environmental conditions at observed species locations to the full range of conditions in a study area to identify unsampled or underrepresented habitats. Adapted to corpus linguistics/NLP, we define a multidimensional 'feature space' for a target domain (e.g., using topic distributions, syntactic complexity, register markers, or semantic vectors). The existing corpus is treated as a set of sample points in this space. By modeling the corpus's coverage of this space and comparing it to a reference model of the domain's full feature distribution (constructed from domain theory, metadata, or a broad but shallow reference set), we can systematically identify underrepresented regions (e.g., specific topics or styles), quantify representativeness, and generate stratified sampling targets to fill gaps, moving beyond simple word-frequency checks.

ideas.title: Relax the focus on static snapshot corpora
ideas.description: Investigate continuously updated, dynamic training corpora that evolve with new information instead of fixed snapshots.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research

ideas.title: Task-Agnostic Data Recipe Inference via Dynamic Early-Exit Signatures
ideas.description: This research proposes to use the early-exit behavior of a model across diverse NLP tasks (sequence labeling, QA, generation) as a novel, multi-faceted signature for reverse-engineering its likely data composition. By analyzing at which layers a model confidently produces correct outputs for different task types, we can infer which data domains and task formats were most influential during training. This creates a synergistic loop: the broader task evaluation provides a rich signal for the inverse problem, while the inferred data recipe explains the observed early-exit patterns, offering a unified framework for model auditing and efficient architecture design.

ideas.title: Apply computational ecology methods to dataset analysis
ideas.description: Use biodiversity metrics to quantify and balance the 'species' of languages and data sources in corpora.
ideas.sourceTitle: BLOOM: A 176B-Parameter Open-Access Multilingual Language Model

ideas.title: Create a dynamic, streaming corpus

ideas.title: The Coherence-Ablation Framework: Isolating the Effects of Source Quality vs. Diversity
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that merges controlled single-source experiments with systematic ablation. Instead of just removing broad categories, the toolkit is designed to curate and ablate based on quantifiable 'quality' dimensions (e.g., logical consistency, explanatory depth) within and across sources. By training models on sequences of corpora where specific quality dimensions are selectively removed or isolated from otherwise diverse datasets, and comparing them to models trained on single sources exemplifying those dimensions, the study can causally attribute model behaviors—such as reasoning coherence or stylistic drift—to either the presence of high-quality data or the interaction of diverse data types. This enables a principled recipe for dataset design that optimizes for specific model traits.

ideas.title: Coordinated Multilingual Foundational Corpora with Built-in Cross-Lingual Alignment
ideas.description: This approach systematically builds foundational open corpora by prioritizing underrepresented languages not in isolation, but in tightly coordinated pairs or clusters with English. For each core knowledge domain, parallel documents, concepts, and structured data are collected and annotated simultaneously across languages. This creates intrinsically aligned datasets from the start, enabling direct research on cross-lingual transfer, translation, and model performance while ensuring the new corpora are immediately usable within the global ecosystem. The system thus overcomes the isolation risk by making interoperability a foundational design constraint, not a later-stage integration challenge.

ideas.title: Challenge English as the default for open research
ideas.description: Prioritize building foundational open corpora in underrepresented, non-English languages first.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Triage Protocols for Domain Corpus Curation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from conservation biology—the use of structured triage protocols to prioritize limited resources for maximum ecosystem benefit—to the problem of curating specialized domain corpora. In ecology, tools like the Species Recovery Potential framework score species on multiple axes (threat, function, uniqueness, recovery feasibility) to guide conservation actions. Adapted here, the open-sourced data curation toolkit would be extended to implement a 'Document Triage' module. This module would automatically score candidate texts from fields like law or medicine on a similar multi-criteria framework: 'Informational Value' (conceptual density, novelty), 'Quality/Reliability' (source authority, factual integrity), 'Feasibility' (processing cost, licensing clarity), and 'Functional Need' (coverage of curricular gaps in the target domain). The toolkit would then use these scores to prioritize and select documents, enabling the efficient, automated construction of high-quality, domain-specific pretraining corpora that maximize informational return within practical constraints, mirroring how ecologists maximize biodiversity outcomes with limited resources.

ideas.title: Challenge uniform training across all languages in the corpus
ideas.description: Test dynamic, adaptive training schedules that prioritize per-language learning needs.
ideas.sourceTitle: BLOOM: A 176B-Parameter Open-Access Multilingual Language Model

ideas.title: Relax the need for massive, centralized datasets
ideas.description: Explore if efficient learning can reduce dependency on large, energy-intensive data collection and processing.

ideas.title: Develop improved training and evaluation code
ideas.description: Enhance the open-source training frameworks and evaluation toolkits.
ideas.sourceTitle: OLMo: Accelerating the Science of Language Models

ideas.title: Provenance-Aware Early-Exit Networks with End-to-End Data Attribution
ideas.description: This research proposes to jointly train a backbone model with its early-exit classifiers from scratch, while simultaneously learning to attribute the influence of specific training data subsets to each exit point. The synergy creates a model where each early-exit classifier becomes specialized not just for a difficulty level, but for data with certain provenance characteristics (e.g., source, quality, or domain). During inference, the model can trace which training data most influenced the prediction at each exit, allowing it to dynamically select the most trustworthy exit based on the provenance similarity of the input to the training data that shaped that classifier. This results in a system that is both efficient and inherently self-explanatory, linking architectural decisions directly to data lineage.

ideas.title: Study long-term impact of biased filtering on models
ideas.description: Quantify how the disproportionate removal of minority-associated text affects downstream model performance and fairness.
ideas.sourceTitle: Documenting Large Webtext Corpora: A Case Study on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus

ideas.title: Coherent Core with Sparse Generalization Bridges
ideas.description: This research tests a hybrid approach that isolates the benefits of single-source coherence while directly addressing its fatal narrowness. A model is first pre-trained exclusively on a single, meticulously curated source (e.g., a vast scientific textbook corpus) to build a deeply coherent knowledge base and reasoning style. Subsequently, it undergoes sparse, targeted fine-tuning on a small, diverse set of high-quality examples designed to act as 'bridges,' teaching it to map its coherent internal representations to related concepts, different stylistic formats, and out-of-domain queries. The core hypothesis is that this method will yield a model that maintains superior internal consistency compared to models trained on mixed data from the start, while demonstrating significantly better robustness and generalization than a purely single-source model.

ideas.title: Quantifying and Minimally Mitigating Extreme-Risk Subsets in Noisy Web Data for Robust Model Training
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to empirically test the necessity of aggressive filtering by first rigorously measuring the risks present in raw web corpora. The system isolates specific, statistically-defined subsets of data that pose extreme legal, safety, or ethical hazards (e.g., violent content, large-scale PII leaks). It then trains comparative models on datasets where only these extreme-risk subsets are removed or sanitized, while the vast majority of noisy but lower-risk data remains intact. The outcome is a principled, evidence-based filter that maximizes data utility for robustness and creativity while definitively neutralizing the most critical failure modes of completely unfiltered training.

ideas.title: Establish Linguistic Keystone Species to Build Resilient Open Research Ecosystems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that English must be the default starting point for open research corpora by proposing a strategic, ecology-inspired alternative. Drawing an analogy from Conservation Biology, where restoring 'keystone species' is prioritized to catalyze broader ecosystem recovery, we adapt this solution to the research context. The adapted idea is to identify and prioritize a select few 'linguistic keystone' languages—chosen for their structural, demographic, or geographic centrality within language families or regions—for the initial development of rich, open foundational corpora. This targeted investment would create high-quality anchors and reusable pipelines, thereby enabling more efficient and sustainable downstream expansion to a wider array of underrepresented languages, ultimately building a more diverse and resilient global research ecosystem.

ideas.title: Investigate other pretrained models
ideas.description: Apply the same experimental methodology to other pretrained language models like RoBERTa or XLNet.
ideas.sourceTitle: Fine-Tuning Pretrained Language Models: Weight Initializations, Data Orders, and Early Stopping

ideas.title: Scalable Data Provenance Auditing via Signature Detection and Hypothesis Testing
ideas.description: This research develops a practical framework for auditing whether specific, pre-identified data subsets (e.g., copyrighted content, demographic groups) have a detectable influence on a trained model's behavior, without requiring exhaustive per-example attribution. The core method involves generating compact, feature-based signatures for data subsets and training small, targeted 'probe' models to detect these signatures within the activations and outputs of the large model under audit. This enables efficient, legally and ethically actionable testing of specific data provenance hypotheses, moving beyond theoretically fragile and computationally impossible exact attribution towards a tool usable for real-world model governance and compliance.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Provenance and Influence Mapping for Heterogeneous Data Sources
ideas.description: This system constructs a network of relationships between data sources by analyzing multiple implicit signals of influence and derivation, rather than relying on explicit citations. It employs natural language processing to detect semantic similarity and shared factual entities, temporal analysis to infer potential precedence, and pattern recognition for structural replication. The output is a weighted, directed graph that models probabilistic data lineage and influence, providing a robust map of the corpus's information ecology where formal citations do not exist.

ideas.title: Causal Data Cartography: A Framework for Ablation-Based Data Quality Metric Validation
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that integrates systematic data ablation with advanced quality assessment. It proposes training a series of models on corpora with specific sources ablated, then using the resulting performance differentials as ground truth to test and calibrate novel data quality metrics. The core innovation is treating metric validation as a causal inference problem, where a metric's true utility is its ability to predict which data removals will most impact model capabilities. This enables the creation of 'causally-grounded' metrics that move beyond correlative heuristics to capture the actual functional contribution of data subsets to emergent model behaviors.

ideas.title: Use released data for further analysis
ideas.description: Leverage the publicly released training and validation scores to build predictive models of fine-tuning success.
ideas.sourceTitle: Fine-Tuning Pretrained Language Models: Weight Initializations, Data Orders, and Early Stopping

ideas.title: Causal Mediation Toolkit for Isolating Training Data Source Effects
ideas.description: This toolkit implements a causal inference approach to isolate the effects of specific training data sources without the confounds of retraining on non-additive, ablated corpora. It uses a fixed, pre-trained model and techniques like representation patching and gradient-based feature attribution to simulate counterfactual scenarios where the influence of a particular data source is removed from the model's internal computations. This allows for a cleaner, more direct measurement of a source's causal contribution to model capabilities and biases. The system outputs quantitative estimates of source-specific effects on model behaviors, controlling for the complex interactions present in the original training mixture.

ideas.title: Assume non-English web content is inherently noisy
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that non-English text requires more aggressive cleaning than English text.
ideas.sourceTitle: Documenting Large Webtext Corpora: A Case Study on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus

ideas.title: Active Domain Discovery and Coverage Assessment via Adaptive Sampling
ideas.description: This system treats the target domain as an unknown structure to be discovered, not a known population to be matched. It begins by using the existing corpus to generate an initial, data-driven model of potential domain strata (e.g., via thematic or conceptual clustering). An active sampling engine then strategically queries external sources—including web APIs, digital libraries, and expert input—to test the completeness of these strata and identify unsampled regions. The process iteratively refines the domain model and provides a quantifiable measure of corpus coverage relative to the discovered domain structure, alongside explicit documentation of uncovered gaps.

ideas.title: Data Quality as a Prior for Instance-Specific Model Complexity
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the concepts by proposing that the intrinsic complexity of a data instance for a model is not a fixed property but is contingent on the quality and characteristics of the pretraining data from which the model was derived. We develop novel, fine-grained data quality metrics—assessing aspects like semantic density, factual consistency, and representational typicality within the training manifold—that serve as priors. These priors are then used to define a conditional complexity function, predicting the uncertainty or learning difficulty a standard model will have with a new instance, based on how well its features align with high-quality regions of the original training data. This allows for proactive data curation, curriculum learning design, and model capacity allocation informed by a predictive understanding of how data quality propagates into task-specific complexity.

ideas.title: Domain-Representative Confidence Calibration for Early-Exit Models
ideas.description: This research fuses survey sampling for corpus assessment with confidence calibration to improve early-exit neural networks. It uses stratified sampling techniques to audit a corpus and identify underrepresented subpopulations within a domain. It then develops calibration techniques that explicitly account for this representativeness profile, ensuring that confidence scores—and the resulting exit decisions—are not overconfident on poorly represented data slices. This yields a system where the reliability of an early prediction is calibrated not just on average, but with respect to the known coverage of the target domain.

ideas.title: A Data Ablation Toolkit with Ecological Tracer and Knockout Methods
ideas.description: This research expands the original data ablation toolkit idea by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, to understand a component's role, scientists use tracer studies (like isotopic labeling) to track its flow and knockout experiments to see system behavior without it. We adapt this to create a dual-method data ablation toolkit. It combines 'data knockout'—systematically training models on corpora with specific source categories removed—with novel 'data tracer' methods. These tracer methods would involve strategically injecting identifiable, synthetic data points or applying mechanistic interpretability techniques to track how information from a specific data category propagates through the model's learning and influences its outputs. This combined approach provides a more complete picture, isolating effects via ablation while actively tracing the pathways of influence, offering deeper causal insight into training data composition effects on model scaling and performance.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Contact Tracing to Data Provenance Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method of epidemiological contact tracing to the problem of mapping relationships between data sources. In epidemiology, contact tracing models identify index cases and map transmission pathways through a population network to understand influence and spread. The analogous solution is adapted by treating each data source in a corpus as a node in a network, with edges defined by shared data fields, transformation dependencies, or direct references (like citations). Using algorithms from epidemiological network analysis (e.g., diffusion models, centrality measures), we can trace the provenance and propagation of data elements, identify foundational or 'source zero' data sources that have outsized influence, and map the overall 'contagion' pattern of data flow and dependency. This provides a dynamic, influence-based map of the corpus's data ecosystem, revealing critical sources and pathways.

ideas.title: Adapt survey sampling for corpus representativeness assessment
ideas.description: Use stratified sampling and weighting techniques from survey methodology to evaluate how well a corpus represents a domain.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dolma: an Open Corpus of Three Trillion Tokens for Language Model Pretraining Research

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune System-Inspired Continuous Corpus Training
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that AI models must be trained on fixed, static snapshot corpora. By drawing an analogy from immunology—specifically the vertebrate adaptive immune system—we propose a novel training paradigm. The analogous solution involves a biological system that continuously updates its defense repertoire through mechanisms like somatic hypermutation and clonal selection in response to new pathogens, all while maintaining a stable memory of past encounters. Adapted to machine learning, this inspires a framework where a model is built with a dual-component architecture: a stable core trained on a verified foundational corpus (the 'innate system') and a dynamic, continuously updated component (the 'adaptive system') that ingests streaming data. New information triggers targeted, efficient updates—similar to an immune response—where only relevant model parameters are adjusted based on a 'challenge' evaluation, preventing catastrophic forgetting and ensuring the model evolves seamlessly with new information without requiring massive, periodic retraining on static snapshots.

ideas.title: Relax assumption of pre-defined key-value splits

ideas.title: Ecological Metrics for Multidimensional Data Quality Profiling
ideas.description: This research develops a suite of metrics that adapt and extend ecological diversity indices (like Shannon, Simpson, and functional diversity indices) to create multidimensional profiles of pretraining data mixtures. These profiles simultaneously measure source diversity, per-source quality (e.g., noise, coherence), and the distribution of functional data characteristics (e.g., reasoning types, linguistic styles, factual domains). The synergy enables the creation of a 'data ecology map' that predicts how different mixtures will influence model performance, robustness, and bias, moving beyond descriptive assessment to prescriptive optimization of data composition for targeted AI capabilities.

ideas.title: Inverse-Designed Early Exits: Jointly Training Models to Reveal and Optimize for Latent Data Recipes
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to jointly train a backbone model with early exit classifiers while simultaneously using the evolving behavior and confidence signatures of these exits as an observational lens. By applying inverse problem methodologies to the exit distributions and their gradients during training, we aim to infer the latent data composition and difficulty spectrum the model is effectively learning from. This inferred 'data recipe' then informs a feedback loop to adaptively reweight the training data or adjust the exit architecture, creating a co-design cycle where the model's internal early-exit structure is optimized for the discovered data hierarchy. The synergy produces a system that not only accelerates inference but also automatically diagnoses and adapts to the core data characteristics that drive its own performance.

ideas.title: The Single-Strain Fermentation Approach to Model Coherence
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that more sources are better by drawing an analogy from microbiology and fermentation science. In that field, a pure, single-strain starter culture is used to ensure a predictable, high-quality, and coherent final product, unlike variable wild fermentations. The adapted research idea is to apply this 'single-strain' principle by training a language model on a single, ultra-high-quality, and internally consistent source (e.g., a complete academic library or a unified codebase). The study would then rigorously evaluate if this 'pure culture' training yields a model with superior coherence—measured by narrative consistency, logical argument flow, and stylistic uniformity—compared to a model trained on a diverse mixture of data of equivalent total size, challenging the prevailing diversity-centric training paradigm.

ideas.title: Dynamic Data-Conditional Early Exits for Petabyte-Stream Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops a large-scale neural network with intermediate classifiers at many layers, designed to process continuous, high-volume data streams. The model dynamically routes each data element, choosing to exit early via a non-adjacent classifier for simple or redundant content, or to engage deeper computation for novel, complex, or high-value samples. This selective processing acts as an intrinsic, real-time curation filter, drastically reducing the computational cost of ingesting the stream while automatically identifying and preserving the most informative data for training or archival. The system thus unifies adaptive inference and large-scale data curation into a single, efficient, end-to-end framework.

ideas.title: Archival Early Exits: A Provenance-Aware Framework for Adaptive Transformer Inference
ideas.description: This research integrates archival science principles for data provenance directly into the adaptive inference mechanisms of early-exit transformer architectures (e.g., RoBERTa, XLNet, GPT). The system treats each internal classifier or exit point as a provenance node, automatically generating and preserving a tamper-evident record of the data lineage, model version, and computational path taken for each prediction. This creates a transparent, auditable chain of custody for AI decisions, crucial for high-stakes or regulated domains, while maintaining the efficiency benefits of early exiting. The result is a transformer that is both computationally adaptive and intrinsically accountable for its reasoning trace.

ideas.title: Create more nuanced personalization evaluation metrics

ideas.title: Mitigating Model Bias in Data-Efficient Learning via Decoupled Exploration and Exploitation
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework to overcome the confirmation bias inherent in standard active learning by decoupling the discovery of informative data from its exploitation for task performance. A separate exploration module, incentivized for discovering novelty and model uncertainty rather than immediate accuracy, scouts the data landscape using Bayesian neural networks and clustering in unsupervised feature spaces. The main learner then performs efficient, targeted training on these curated, high-potential data batches, with safeguards against catastrophic forgetting. This approach ensures the system does not get trapped in its initial biases and can achieve high performance with minimal data by guaranteeing exposure to the most broadly informative examples.

ideas.title: Invert the assumption that more sources are better

ideas.title: Versioned Dynamic Corpora with Stability-Preserving Integration
ideas.description: This research investigates a framework for dynamic training corpora that rigorously prevents catastrophic forgetting and uncontrolled drift. The system maintains a versioned, auditable knowledge base where new information is integrated through controlled updates against a preserved foundation of core knowledge. It employs continuous multi-dimensional evaluation to monitor for regressions in reasoning, factual accuracy, and safety alignment. The outcome is a methodology for models to evolve safely with new information while maintaining stable, predictable performance on established tasks.

ideas.title: Apply code-switching linguistics to model training

ideas.title: Causal Audit of Data Curation via Controlled Multi-Arm Long-Term Training Experiments
ideas.description: This research establishes a causal understanding of data curation effects by conducting parallel, controlled long-term training experiments. A cohort of models, identical in architecture and initialization, are trained on a shared base dataset, but with systematically varied curation 'treatments' applied to specific data segments in a factorial design. This methodology isolates the impact of individual filtering and mixing decisions on final model capabilities, biases, and safety failures, moving beyond correlation to identify direct causal mechanisms. The resulting framework produces a detailed map of how curation choices steer model development, enabling the design of safer, more predictable data pipelines.

ideas.title: Empirically-Grounded Data Quality Metrics via Proxy Model Performance Prediction
ideas.description: This research develops data quality assessment metrics through a rigorous, empirical validation pipeline rather than theoretical construction. Candidate metrics are used to score and filter pretraining data subsets, which are then used to train small-scale proxy models. The performance of these proxy models on a comprehensive benchmark suite provides direct, causal evidence for which metrics are predictive of downstream capabilities. The final output is a small set of validated, interpretable metrics, along with a public framework for continuously testing new proposed measures against this empirical standard.

ideas.title: Bayesian Attribution of Model Characteristics to Plausible Data Generation Processes
ideas.description: This research develops a method to infer the probable data composition and filtering steps that led to a trained model by treating it as a Bayesian inference problem. Instead of seeking a single deterministic 'recipe,' the system generates a distribution over plausible data-generation processes, constrained by priors from documented ML practices and causal tests. It actively probes the model with synthetic counterfactual data to establish which model attributes are robustly caused by specific data properties. The output is a set of candidate data recipes with associated confidence scores, enabling auditors to prioritize the most likely explanations for a model's behavior without assuming a unique solution.
